title: Theory-Guided Modeling for Network Performance Prediction
authors: Luyu Qi, Yulei Wu, Dimitra Simeonidou
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network Science and Engineering
abstract: The rapid evolution of wireless communication technologies has dramatically increased the complexity of network environments, making accurate performance prediction a critical task for network planning and optimization. Reliable prediction supports efficient resource management, quality of service assurance, and robust system operation. However, current approaches face three key challenges: (i) the wireless propagation environment is highly complex and difficult to model, (ii) measurement data are costly to collect and often imbalanced, and (iii) the lack of interpretability and credibility in data-driven models. To address these issues, we propose a Theory-Guided Modeling (TGM) framework that integrates communication theory with machine learning. TGM embeds classical propagation laws such as the log-distance path loss model and Shannon capacity into its architecture and loss function, introduces theoretical priors to enhance robustness under imperfect data, and outputs interpretable physical coefficients constrained by partial differential equations (PDEs). This hybrid paradigm effectively balances accuracy, robustness, and interpretability. Comprehensive experiments on path-loss and throughput prediction show that TGM achieves up to 62.6% reduction in mean absolute error (MAE) compared with state-of-the-art methods.

title: Performance and Efficiency Trade-Off Analysis of Modern CNN Architectures for Automated Stem Cell Colony Quality Assessment_supp1-3692455.pdf
authors: Hatice Hilal GURLEYEN
publish: N/A

title: Federated Learning with Enhanced Privacy via Model Splitting and Random Client Participation
authors: Yiwei Li, Shuai Wang, Zhuojun Tian, Xiuhua Wang, Shijian Su
publish: IEEE Transactions on Cognitive Communications and Networking
abstract: Federated Learning (FL) often adopts differential privacy (DP) to protect client data, but the added noise required for privacy guarantees can substantially degrade model accuracy. To resolve this challenge, we propose model-splitting privacy-amplified federated learning (MS-PAFL), a novel framework that combines structural model splitting with statistical privacy amplification. In this framework, each client’s model is partitioned into a private submodel, retained locally, and a public submodel, released for global aggregation. The calibrated Gaussian noise is injected only into the public submodel, thereby confining its adverse impact while preserving the utility of the retained local model components. We further present a rigorous theoretical analysis that characterizes the joint privacy amplification achieved through random client participation and local data subsampling under this architecture. The analysis provides explicit bounds on both single-round and total privacy loss, demonstrating that MS-PAFL significantly reduces the noise necessary to satisfy a target privacy protection level. Extensive experiments validate our theoretical findings, showing that MS-PAFL consistently attains a superior privacy–utility trade-off compared with conventional DP-FL baselines and enables the training of highly accurate models under strong privacy guarantees.

title: End-to-End Vectorized HD Map Construction Based on Graph Structure Modeling and Graph Transformer Optimization
authors: Wenjing Bai, Yunzhou Zhang, Qi Guo, Wei Liu, Zuotao Ning, Shuai Cheng, Chaoran Yang, Li Song
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: High-definition (HD) maps play a crucial role in autonomous driving by providing a reliable foundation for behavior prediction and path planning. Recent methods model map elements as point sets and employ Transformer-based detection frameworks for end-to-end vectorized map construction. However, these approaches do not fully exploit geometric structural properties, thereby limiting the accuracy of predicting the shape and position of map elements. To address this limitation, we propose a novel method called GraphMapTR, which models map elements as graph structures. Each map element is represented as a subgraph where key points serve as nodes and edges capture structural continuity. To optimize the node query embeddings within subgraphs, we design a Graph Transformer-based decoder comprising three core components: Multi-head Self-Attention Between Subgraphs (MHSA-BSG), which facilitates efficient feature interaction across different subgraphs while enhancing node attention to their respective subgraph regions; Local Geometric-aware Self-Attention Within Subgraphs (LGSA-WSG), which dynamically aggregates global node relationships and local edge structures to enrich node representations; and Deformable Cross-Attention (DCA-BEV), which refines node embeddings through interaction with BEV features. In LGSA-WSG, priors from the rasterized map segmentation branch are incorporated to generate local geometric consistency scores (LGC-scores), which constitute edge structural bias. Extensive experiments demonstrate that GraphMapTR significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods. In particular, it outperforms the state-of-the-art algorithm MapTRv2 by 5.0% mAP and 3.4% mAP on the nuScenes and Argoverse2 datasets, respectively.

title: Cooperative Edge Caching with Large Language Model in Wireless Networks_supp1-3687739.pdf
authors: Ning Yang
publish: N/A
abstract: Cooperative edge caching within spatially overlapping coverage zones introduces intricate coupling among Base Station (BS) caching decisions, rendering online content replacement highly sensitive to both spatial topology and temporal content reuse. Conventional heuristics often suffer from myopia, while Deep Reinforcement Learning agents typically rely on brittle numerical state/action representations and necessitate computationally prohibitive retraining under topological or traffic dynamics. This paper studies a centralized, cooperative multi-BS cache-replacement controller driven by a Large Language Model (LLM) within a deterministic text-to-action execution loop. At each time slot, the global cache state is deterministically rendered into a prompt encapsulating the cache inventory of each BS, the current set of deduplicated requests, and multi-scale frequency summaries. The LLM generates exactly one decision line per BS, after which a strict parser and feasibility checker either accept a feasible joint action or deterministically fall back to the all-BS no-operation (NoOp) action. We align the LLM through a two-stage training paradigm: initially performing completion-only Supervised Fine-Tuning on look-ahead expert trajectories to acquire action syntax and robust initialization, followed by Group Relative Policy Optimization. The latter employs an “opportunity-aware” reward mechanism, utilizing the multi-step cooperative hit rate gain relative to a NoOp baseline as the primary signal, together with an explicit invalid-output penalty and an annealed early-stage NoOp correction that prevents degenerate passivity. We focus on a reactive replacement setting with equal-sized files, at most one replacement per BS per slot, and insertion candidates restricted to current-slot requests. Under a frozen-trajectory evaluation protocol that reuses identical request traces and association graphs across methods, the proposed orchestrator approaches a single-step exhaustive-search reference (0.610 vs. 0.617 in a standard 5-BS scenario), surpasses classical baselines (e.g., +4.1% over least-frequently used), and exhibits robust zero-shot transfer across cache capacity, library size, popularity skewness, and user density. Code is available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/gracefulning/CoopLLM-Cache</uri>.

title: High-Gain End-Fire Antenna Based on Slow-Wave Substrate-Integrated Waveguide Loading
authors: Hailu Jin, Xiaorong Jing, Ping Wang, Haiyan Jin, Guixue Liang, Feixiang Huang
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: In conventional series-fed substrate-integrated waveguide (SIW) end-fire antenna arrays, the realized gain is often limited by the feed-to-radiation phase mismatch between the guided excitation and the free-space end-fire phase. To address this issue, polyline-loaded slow-wave SIW sections are inserted between adjacent radiating elements to tailor the inter-element guided phase progression, thereby enabling gain-enhanced end-fire radiation. For verification, a five-element deformed Yagi–Uda array based on an SIW traveling-wave line is designed, fabricated, and measured. The antenna features a compact transverse cross section of 0.15λ<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0</sub> × 0.06λ<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0</sub> Measured results show a usable end-fire band of 13.75–14.25 GHz, within which the reflection coefficient is below −10 dB, the beam remains end-fire, the average radiation efficiency is 75%, and the front-to-back ratio (FBR) exceeds 15 dB. At 14.25 GHz, the antenna achieves a peak gain of 15.2 dBi.

title: Reinforcement Learning based Energy Management Strategy for Hybrid Renewable Micro Grid with EV Integration
authors: Karanam Deepak, G Pandu Ranga Reddy, S Raj Kumar, Bondili Jithendra Singh, Jadala Aravind Kumar, Pasupala Jayanth
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Electronic Devices and Intelligent Systems (ICSEDIS)
abstract: The rise of renewable energy and electric vehicles (EV) has led to new operational difficulties for today's power systems. Intelligent energy management strategies are imperative for integrating renewable microgrids powered by PV, wind, and batteries to ensure stability, economy, and efficiency in the use of electric energy resources. Traditional techniques used for optimization are not able to account for the variabilities associated with renewable sources of energy and EV charging requirements. An energy management approach is proposed based on the use of reinforcement learning (RL) for hybrid renewable microgrids with EVs. In this method, the operation of the microgrid is formulated as a Markov Decision Process (MDP) in order to facilitate the learning of energy dispatch policies through an RL agent. Using the proposed model, renewable energy generation, battery storage systems, EV charging demand, and grid interaction can all be managed together. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method can increase the efficiency of renewable energy utilization, reduce operational costs, and improve system reliability compared with traditional rule-based control techniques.

title: An Intelligent Machine Learning Approach for Wind Turbine Fault Detection
authors: N. Bhupesh Kumar, Rajesh Thangaraj, Kandukuri Kumari, K. Kiran Kumar, Imran Abdul, D. Chandra Sekhar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Electronic Devices and Intelligent Systems (ICSEDIS)
abstract: As the world's need for clean electricity continues to rise, wind power is quickly becoming a viable and long-term option. On the other hand, wind turbines are susceptible to many problems since they are complicated electromechanical devices that function in very dynamic and unpredictable environments. Reduced energy efficiency, unplanned maintenance, catastrophic damage, and expensive downtime are all consequences of crucial component failures such the gearbox, generator's bearings, & blades. Failure to adapt to changing fault patterns or notice tiny changes in turbine performance data render’s traditional fault detection methods inadequate. These methods include threshold-based monitoring, vibration assessment with defined criteria, and periodic physical inspections. To guarantee the dependability and lifespan of wind turbines in massive amounts wind farms, more sophisticated, automated, and intelligent problem detection technologies are urgently required.

title: An Intelligent Distributed Base Station Deployment Strategy for Low-Altitude UAV Sensing
authors: Lichao Zhu, Bo An, Liang Wu, Zaichen Zhang
publish: IEEE Wireless Communications Letters
abstract: In the context of a developing low-altitude communication and sensing, the distribution of unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) is characterised by dynamism and heterogeneity, posing a substantial challenge to conventional wireless network planning methodologies. Conventional metrics, such as mean squared error (MSE), which are predicated on point-to-point errors, are less efficacious in evaluating the overall congruence between the network service distribution and the highly concentrated, irregular patterns of UAV activity. This letter proposes an alternative paradigm for the distributed base station deployment, which is framed as an information-theoretic task. The fundamental premise is to minimize the Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence between the target UAV activity distribution and the network service distribution. To investigate this concept, a deep reinforcement learning (DRL) agent is proposed, and it autonomously adjusts the spatial positions of distributed base stations to minimize the KL divergence. This proposed strategy enables a more precise and efficient alignment of network resources with the real-time UAV demand, significantly enhancing the coverage efficiency and the service quality in dynamic environments.

title: Distributed CPS-Based Predefined-Time Control of Large-Scale Plug-and-Play Inverter Air Conditioner Clusters for Demand Response
authors: Fan-Rong Qu, Zhi-Wei Liu, Yan-Wu Wang, Jia-Kai Wu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Cyber-Physical Systems
abstract: Industrial cyber-physical systems (CPS) increasingly rely on coordinated flexible loads to enhance grid stability and renewable energy integration. Among these, inverter air conditioners (IACs), as networked CPS agents, offer significant potential for demand response (DR) applications. This paper studies the predefined-time coordination of large-scale IAC clusters and proposes a CPS-based distributed control framework. By coupling the power tracking objective with user comfort consensus, the proposed approach coordinately regulates the power variation rates of individual air-conditioning units. This integrated design enables fast and accurate tracking of power commands at the system level within a predefined time, while simultaneously ensuring fairness in user comfort across the cluster. Rigorous convergence is established via Lyapunov-based stability analysis. To accommodate the plug-and-play nature of IACs, where units may dynamically join or leave the DR process, an adaptive shared-variable mechanism is further proposed to preserve network connectivity and coordination performance under dynamic topology variations. Extensive simulation studies demonstrate that the proposed framework effectively mitigates demand fluctuations and achieves rapid response to DR signals within the predefined time.

title: HFAVL: Hard-label Fusion Attack toward Vision-language Model
authors: Hongbo Cao, Yongqi Sun, Li Duan, Yifan Sui, Xisu Wang
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: With the advancement of artificial intelligence, vision-language models (VLMs) that integrate text and image modalities have become central to multimodal learning and are increasingly deployed in Internet of Things (IoT) environments such as smart surveillance, autonomous driving and industrial monitoring. However, VLMs are also highly susceptible to adversarial attacks. Most previous black-box attack methods towards the VLMs rely on either the soft label or substitute models. However, most of them need detailed information on the target model, which is often unavailable for real-world threat models due to limitations on additional access and resource restrictions. In this study, we propose a surrogate-free hard-label attack towards the VLMs, which does not require a substitute model, called Hard-Label Fusion Attack towards Vision-Language models. HFAVL directly leverages the model’s final decision to jointly generate the perturbation for both text and image modalities instead of the confidence score necessary for the soft label attack. By transforming the text into a continuous embedding space that enables them to be aligned with the image in a unified manner, HFAVL applies cooperative Monte Carlo-based estimation on the multimodal gradient. Aiming to improve the efficiency of attacks, we introduce a Mutual Iterative Refinement mechanism (MIR), which gradually searches for a better adversarial example through small iterative steps that blend perturbations across both modalities. Additionally, to alleviate the imbalance between textual and visual noise during the attack, we propose a new multimodal edit distance evaluation metric to measure the quality of multimodal adversarial examples. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that HFAVL consistently outperforms existing multimodal adversarial attack methods across various vision-language pretraining (VLP) models. Notably, under the setting with recall@10, HFAVL achieves over 80% attack success rate on most benchmarks, demonstrating its efficiency and high precision in generating effective adversarial examples.

title: P-Channel Tri-gate Nanowire InGaN/GaN/AlN/InAlGaN MOS-HFET for Power Switching Applications
authors: Jian-Hong Ke, Ching-Sung Lee, Chung-Liang Liao, Wei-Chou Hsu
publish: IEEE Journal of the Electron Devices Society
abstract: This work reports record-high three-terminal on-state drain-source breakdown voltage (BVDS) of -975 V and superior switching characteristics of p-channel InGaN/GaN/AlN/ InAlGaN metal-oxide-semiconductor heterostructure field-effect transistors (MOS-HFETs) with a tri-gate nanowire structure. Improved gate modulation capability and breakdown performance were achieved by the devised tri-gate nanowire and gate field-plate (GFP) structures. Non-vacuum ultrasonic spray pyrolysis deposition (USPD) technique was used to deposit the high-k wide-bandgap Al2O3 as the gate dielectric and surface passivation simultaneously. The present p-channel tri-gate nanowire MOS-HFET design has exhibited improved maximum drain-source current density (IDS, max) of -28.0 mA/mm, subthreshold swing (SS) of 75.6 mV/dec, on/off current ratio (Ion/Ioff) of 1.1 × 108, two-terminal off-state gate-drain breakdown voltage (BVGD) of 995 V, BVDS of -975 V, and Baliga’s figure of merit (BFOM) of 60.5 MW/cm2. The reliability of the present design was also evaluated through time-dependent electrical stress, demonstrating its advantageous potential for high-voltage complementary power-switching circuit applications.

title: Megahertz Soft-Switching Wireless Power Transfer for Capsule Robots Under Dynamic Position and Orientation Variations_supp2-3693630.avi
authors: Chi Kwan Lee
publish: N/A
abstract: Capsule robots for gastrointestinal (GI) diagnostics have gained increasing attention in recent years owing to their miniature form factor and non-invasive, painless operation. However, their limited size restricts the integration of large batteries, which poses a major challenge when advanced functions such as high-frame-rate, high-resolution video recording are required. Wireless power transfer (WPT) offers a promising solution to this limitation. In this work, we propose an MHz-range WPT scheme that leverages the high-Q characteristics of coils at MHz frequencies, enabling a significant reduction in both transmitter and receiver coil sizes, which is essential for miniaturized capsule robots. Furthermore, a three-dimensional receiver coil structure is analyzed and compared, identifying configurations with improved efficiency. A key challenge in capsule WPT is that capsule motion through the digestive tract alters the reflected impedance seen by the Class-E driver, thereby disrupting its zero-voltage switching (ZVS) operation. To address this issue, two control methods are introduced to ensure consistent soft-switching under varying capsule orientations. In addition, a power regulation algorithm is developed to stabilize the output voltage and its performance is experimentally validated. Finally, the average received power along the entire GI trajectory is evaluated under a constant transmitter current. These results establish a foundation for the development of next-generation wirelessly powered capsule robots.

title: 3D-Printed Folded Transmitarray with Gain-Filtering Response and Low Cross-Polarization
authors: Yuanxi Cao, Le Chang, Sifan Wu, Jianxing Li, Juan Chen, Sen Yan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: An air-filled cavity filter-based folded transmitarray antenna (FTA) is proposed in this communication. The transmitarray (TA) of FTA is realized by rectangular waveguide filters. The varied size of the coupling inductive iris between the resonators enables the filters to realize nearly the same filtering response and the 3-bit phase delay. The bottom plate of the FTA is periodically arranged with 45°-tilted parallel plate waveguides (PPWs), which can out-of-phase reflect the ±45° polarized incident waves, then achieve the polarization conversion. The main contribution of the proposed design is the utilization of transmission and reflection characteristics of rectangular waveguide-based TAs. The concept enables polarization selection in the frequency domain, achieving extremely high reflection coefficients. Moreover, the cutoff characteristic of the TA can effectively suppress the transmission of cross-polarized electromagnetic waves, resulting in low cross-polarization level. To verify the feasibility of the design, an FTA prototype is fabricated using 3D printing technology at the center frequency of 26.0 GHz. The measured results show that the antenna can realize the gain filtering beam with the rejection level around -20.0 dB, and the maximum aperture efficiency of 50.3%. In addition, several defocus sources are used to excite the FTA, demonstrating its multi-beam capability.

title: Path Loss Prediction for Maritime Tropospheric Scatter Based on CNN-MLP
authors: Hengzhi Liu, Kunlei Du
publish: 2026 International Conference on Generative Artificial Intelligence and Information Security (GAIIS)
abstract: Tropospheric scatter is a key method for achieving maritime over-the-horizon communication, and accurate prediction of its path loss is crucial for communication link design. To address the issue that traditional empirical models rely on static climatic parameters and struggle to adapt to the complex and dynamic maritime surface environment, this paper proposes a dual-branch path loss prediction method based on a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and a Multilayer Perceptron (MLP), utilizing measured channel data. The proposed method is compared and analyzed against BP and LSTM networks. Experimental results demonstrate that the CNN-MLP model effectively mines the deep coupling features among input parameters, achieving a prediction Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) of 6.1 dB, which is significantly superior to the comparison methods, thus validating its superiority in predicting maritime tropospheric scatter path loss.

title: Enhanced Encrypted IoT Malicious Traffic Detection via Adaptive Fusion and Focal Loss: An Improved Heterogeneous Graph Approach
authors: Mianzhang Luo, Xiaoyi Yang, Yuqing Lan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Generative Artificial Intelligence and Information Security (GAIIS)
abstract: The rapid proliferation of the Internet of Things (IoT) has significantly expanded the attack surface of network environments, making malicious traffic detection a critical security challenge. The widespread adoption of encryption technologies further exacerbates this challenge, as traditional plaintext feature-based detection methods are no longer effective. Although existing deep learning approaches have shown progress in encrypted traffic classification, they still face limitations in feature fusion strategies and the accurate identification of hard-to-classify samples. To address these shortcomings, this paper proposes an enhanced approach based on a dual-granularity heterogeneous graph neural network framework. Specifically, we introduce an adaptive feature fusion mechanism that dynamically adjusts feature weights and employs cross-view attention to fully exploit the complementarity of features across different granularities. Additionally, we replace the conventional cross-entropy loss with Focal Loss, enabling the model to focus more on challenging MitM (Man-in-the-Middle) samples during training. Extensive experiments conducted on the CIC-IIoT 2025 and IoT-23 datasets demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves significant improvements in both weighted average accuracy and F1 score over baseline methods. Compared to the state-of-the-art encrypted traffic classification framework, MH-Net, the F1 score improves by 11.5% on the CIC-IIoT dataset, while also significantly reducing the misclassification of MitM samples as benign traffic. These results confirm the effectiveness and superiority of the proposed method. This work provides a practical solution for encrypted traffic classification in IoT environments, enabling more accurate detection of malicious traffic and improving the security of IoT networks.

title: A Single-Tail CMOS Comparator for High-Speed and Low-Power Data Conversion
authors: K.Lokesh Krishna, M.Kalpana, K. Madhavi, Kanipakam Yaswanth, Kasim Kuppam Sushmitha, Lomada Bhanneswar Reddy
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Electronic Devices and Intelligent Systems (ICSEDIS)
abstract: Comparators are essential building blocks in high-speed data converters and mixed-signal integrated circuits, particularly in flash analog-to-digital converters, where strict requirements on power efficiency, conversion speed, and circuit area must be met. This work describes the design and realization of a single-tail CMOS comparator tailored for high-speed and energy-efficient operation. The proposed topology adopts a dynamically operated latch with a single tail device, which limits transistor stacking and reduces dynamic power consumption while preserving strong regenerative feedback for rapid decision making. The comparator is implemented and evaluated using Cadence Virtuoso tool in 90nm CMOS technology with a 1.8V power supply (V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">DD</inf>). Transient analysis simulations are conducted using differential input signals biased around mid-supply and clocked in the nanosecond regime. The simulation results exhibit rail-to-rail output voltage transitions (from zero to 1.8 volts), reduced propagation delay on the order of nanoseconds, and markedly lower dynamic power dissipation relative to conventional designs, demonstrating the suitability of the proposed single-tail comparator for high-speed, low-power flash ADC applications.

title: Controllable Echocardiography Video Generation for Safe Medical Data Sharing
authors: Jinduo Wang, Fang Zhou
publish: 2026 International Conference on Generative Artificial Intelligence and Information Security (GAIIS)
abstract: Echocardiography is a key non-invasive imaging modality for cardiac assessment, yet its analysis is limited by noise, operator dependency, and the lack of large-scale annotated video data. Moreover, privacy concerns restrict data sharing across institutions. To address these challenges, we propose a generative-model-based framework for privacy-preserving echocardiographic data sharing. The model is trained offline to learn the distribution of real echocardiography videos and is released instead of raw data, enabling the synthesis of realistic and diverse cardiac sequences. By incorporating physiologically controllable signals, the proposed method generates semantically meaningful cardiac dynamics while protecting patient privacy. Experiments show that models trained on synthetic data achieve competitive performance on real echocardiographic data. This work offers a practical solution for scalable and secure echocardiographic analysis.

title: Improving Fairness in Airline Integrated Crew Scheduling - Colleague Familiarity and Team Experience Distribution Considerations
authors: Tianyu Wangs, Hoi-Lam Ma, Xin Wen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Engineering Management
abstract: Psychological health of flight crew is critical to flight safety. Due to job nature (e.g., irregular work schedules, frequent jet lag), colleagues can sometimes outweigh the importance of family for flight crew. Thus, building colleague familiarity and workplace friendship is crucial. However, under the existing air crew scheduling (ACS) approach, crew members face frequent colleague changes and have to work with strangers, causing poor work environments. Besides, reasonable experience level distributions in crew teams are also crucial for crew satisfaction. However, the existing ACS approach frequently produces crew teams with unbalanced distributions of experience levels (e.g., all members from various ranks have lower-end experience levels within the qualified range). To overcome these drawbacks, this study develops a novel multi-objective ACS approach that can improve colleague familiarity (i.e., increase the chance for crew members to work together) and form crew teams with more reasonable and balanced experience level distributions. An evolutionary algorithm that incorporates the hyperplane separation and heuristic search strategy is proposed to derive Pareto solutions. The proposed ACS approach is examined on long-haul flight schedules which impose more complicated crew work/rest regulations. Extensive computational experiments with subsequent analysis validate the proposed method's effectiveness, showing an impressive improvement in colleague familiarity (1989.45%,

title: Proactive Traffic Surge Forecasting for Student Result Portals using LSTM Networks
authors: Dhyana SK, Dharshana Priya V, Alexander
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Electronic Devices and Intelligent Systems (ICSEDIS)
abstract: Student result portals face extreme and predictable traffic surges immediately following examination result declarations, placing sudden strain on server infrastructure that standard reactive management strategies cannot adequately address. This study proposes a proactive traffic forecasting framework using Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks to predict near-future user volumes on academic result portals before surges occur. The system ingests sequential traffic measurements collected at one-minute intervals, applies Min-Max normalization and temporal feature engineering, and generates multi-step-ahead forecasts covering a 10–30 minute horizon. Experimental evaluation conducted across multiple result-release cycles demonstrates that the LSTM model achieves a Mean Absolute Error of 118, an RMSE of 174, a MAPE of 4.92%, and a Peak Timing Error of just 2 minutes — substantially outperforming naïve persistence, moving average, and ARIMA baselines. These results confirm that sequential demand forecasting enables system administrators to initiate autoscaling and load-balancing operations well in advance of congestion, improving portal availability during the most critical academic events.

title: AI-Powered Multimodal Human Detection System Combining YOLOv11 and Terrain Data in Search and Rescue Operations
authors: Sangeetha Archana J, Mohanraj K, Monishkumar S, Sanjay S
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Electronic Devices and Intelligent Systems (ICSEDIS)
abstract: Rapid identification and prioritization of victims can bring tremendous value to rescue operations. So far, the existing methods rely only on vision-based detection, often yielding broad results with no risk-based prioritization. In this paper, a deep learning-based rescue aid system is proposed using YOLOv11 for target detection, trained on diverse incident scene datasets, namely Incidents1M, and fine-tuned using human-focused benchmarks, C2A. This novelty in vision fusion merges the YOLOv11-based target detection with terrain intelligence categorization through Digital Elevation Model and Inertial Measurement Unit data to offer context-aware rescue decision-making. Based on such extensive training and sensor fusion, the system provides reliable identification of victims even in challenging situations and conveys precise timely information that enhances rescue missions. By applying the methodologies proposed in this work, we were able to obtain an accuracy of 95%. Experimental results shown that YOLOv11 provides highest result in terms of accuracy for human detection on the Incidents1M and C2A dataset.

title: SPCL: Semantic Prototype Contrastive Learning for Incomplete Multimodal Sentiment Analysis
authors: Tiantian Zhang, JiaQi Zhao, Qianwen Han, Fuying Zhao, Zexi Zhang, Xueyuan Cai
publish: 2026 International Conference on Generative Artificial Intelligence and Information Security (GAIIS)
abstract: In the field of artificial intelligence, data incompleteness represents a pervasive and highly challenging issue that severely constrains model performance, leading to information loss, statistical bias, and a significant decline in generalisation capabilities. Existing solutions, such as data deletion or various interpolation techniques, often suffer from inherent flaws including the loss of valuable information, the introduction of secondary noise, or the distortion of the original data distribution. To overcome these limitations, our core idea is to introduce a ‘prototype space’ constructed from vast, high-quality, and clean linguistic data. This space serves as a set of stable and robust semantic anchors. Building upon this, we devise a ‘prototype-guided contrastive learning’ objective. This objective imposes semantic regularisation during model training by aligning the projections of incomplete data in the representation space with corresponding semantic anchors within the prototype space. This work offers a novel perspective on addressing data incompleteness by starting from the representation space.

title: MA-STGNN: Monsoon-Aware Spatiotemporal Graph Neural Networks with Hybrid Attention for Groundwater Forecasting in India
authors: Makanyadevi K, Mounika B R, Elavarasi A, Ivanthika R, Monikashree P
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Electronic Devices and Intelligent Systems (ICSEDIS)
abstract: In India, groundwater depletion is a major agricultural sustainability and water security issue, compounded by the large seasonal variability in water usage associated with the country's monsoons. As such, traditional methods of estimating future groundwater levels fail to account for the interconnectedness of aquifers, and the nonlinear impact of climatic variability on these levels. Consequently, this study proposes a Monsoon-Aware Spatiotemporal Graph Neural Network (MA-STGNN) framework to predict groundwater levels with improved accuracy. By combining graph convolutional layers to capture the spatial relationship of monitoring wells and LSTM layers to model the temporal changes in groundwater levels, the MA-STGNN accurately predicts future groundwater levels. The MA-STGNN also includes an attention mechanism that is aware of seasonal rainfall recharge patterns and lag effects from rainfall. In addition, and in order to improve forecasting accuracy further, the MA-STGNN combines ARIMA-based linear forecasting with graph neural networks to predict the nonlinear residuals of the forecast. Fifteen years of groundwater level data from India-WRIS were utilized in the MA-STGNN experiments, which were conducted in multiple hydrogeological zones across India. The results of the study indicate that the MA-STGNN significantly improved forecasting accuracy over traditional statistical forecasting methods and deep learning methods such as LSTM and Transformer models, as demonstrated by lower RMSE and higher reliability of the predictions. Thus, the MA-STGNN provides a scalable, data-driven tool for assessing the risk of groundwater levels and for sustainably managing water resources.

title: Energy Efficiency and Carbon Reduction Potential of HTTP Caching in ICT-enabled Critical Infrastructure
authors: Sebastián Soler Domínguez, Rajesh K. Ahir, Jagruti Thakur
publish: IEEE Transactions on Green Communications and Networking
abstract: The rapid growth of web services is increasing the energy consumption and carbon footprint of Information and communications technology (ICT) infrastructure. While HTTP caching is widely deployed to improve performance, its quantitative energy and CO2 benefits remain insufficiently characterized. This paper presents a measurement-calibrated framework to estimate the energy use and associated carbon emissions of HTTP content delivery with and without caching. This study introduces a Projection-Calibrated Web Energy-Carbon Calculator (PC-WECC) that maps web workloads to energy and emissions by integrating data-center power usage effectiveness (PUE), network energy intensity, and country-level grid carbon intensity projections through 2030. To capture application-level effects, we develop an Empirically-Fitted Cache Efficiency Regression Model (ECERM) based on controlled server experiments that measure joules-per-gigabyte for cached and non-cached delivery. Results show that HTTP caching is 5−20% more energy efficient than non-cache delivery for typical web objects (< 5MB), with efficiency strongly dependent on file size and protocol overhead. When scaled to realistic traffic volumes, caching can yield substantial emissions reductions; for example, an average news website (∼ 300k daily visits) can avoid approximately 150tCO2 per year. This framework provides a transparent and reproducible pathway to translate HTTP architectural choices into energy and carbon impacts, supporting sustainable design and deployment of web infrastructure.

title: An analytical model for flexible capacitive pressure sensors with hemispherical microstructure-based dielectric layer of high sensing performance_supp1-3691819.docx
authors: Xuefeng Zhang
publish: N/A
abstract: Flexible capacitive pressure sensors are extensively employed in health monitoring and human-machine interaction due to their high sensitivity, simple structure, and low power consumption. Microstructures engineering of the dielectric layer is an effective way for improving the sensing performance and design flexibility of these devices. However, conventional designs of microstructures are usually conducted by Finite element analysis (FEA) and trial-and-error method because of the absence of a powerful theoretical tool. As a result, the complex and inefficient processes hinder the rapid and accurate design of microstructures. In this study, an analytical model with high precision and efficiency for capacitive pressure sensors with hemispherical microstructured dielectric layers is established based on the modified method of dimensionality reduction (MDR) and the hyperelasticity theory by taking the incompressible characteristics of the PDMS into account, which can describe the relationship between the microstructures and the sensing performance precisely and efficiently. Guided by this theoretical model, a capacitive flexible pressure sensor with high sensing performance has been designed. The resulting sensor exhibits a rapid response (70 ms), a low detection limit (~15 Pa), and excellent stability (over 8000 loading/unloading cycles). These capabilities enable the sensor to capture pressure variations induced by human activities with high fidelity, demonstrating its significant potential for applications in wearable biomechanical monitoring and personalized healthcare.

title: Communication-Free CC/CV Phase Shift Control for SS-Compensated WPT System under Misalignment via Reflected Impedance Decoupling
authors: Xiaodong Yang, Ka Wai Eric Cheng, Shuye Shang
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: Accurate constant current (CC) and constant voltage (CV) charging in wireless power transfer (WPT) systems is challenging in practice because coil misalignment is inevitable during operation and the battery equivalent resistance varies nonlinearly during charging. These effects often degrade regulation accuracy and complicated transmitter (Tx) side control, particularly in communication-free systems. This article proposes a Tx communication-free CC/CV control method for series–series compensated WPT systems that remains effective under misalignment. The mutual inductance is identified prior to charging by detuning the receiver to alter reflected impedance, while the equivalent load is estimated online during charging. By decoupling mutual inductance from load variation prior to charging, a unified closed-form phase shift control law enables CC/CV regulation without receiver (Rx) side sensing, communication or auxiliary converters. Experimental results on a 50 W prototype show that mutual inductance estimation errors are below 6.4% and equivalent load errors within 3.1%. Under various coil misalignment conditions, the system maintains within 1% steady state regulation error for both CC and CV operation.

title: TSGNAS: A Topology- and Semantic-Guided Graph Neural Network Architecture Searcher_supp1-3692360.pdf
authors: Shanchen Pang
publish: N/A
abstract: Designing effective graph neural networks (GNNs) for diverse tasks requires substantial manual effort, especially when dealing with the intricate interplay between topological structures and semantic information in graph data. Existing automated approaches often rely on off-the-shelf model combinations that remain superficial, resulting in generated model frameworks that are hardly interpretable and limited by the capabilities of the combined models. To address this gap, we propose an evolutionary-based framework that systematically searches for optimal GNN architectures while adaptively combining topological and semantic insights. This approach reduces human intervention, accommodates a wide range of graph tasks, and ensures robust performance in complex scenarios. Extensive experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate that our method outperforms both handcrafted GNNs and mainstream AutoML models in terms of accuracy, offering a scalable and adaptive solution for GNN design.

title: A Two-Tier Stacking Ensemble Approach for Noise-Resilient Fitness Classification
authors: Arnav Pandey, Rahul Sheoran, Parth Singh Chandel, Yamini B
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Electronic Devices and Intelligent Systems (ICSEDIS)
abstract: Achieving accurate fitness classification from wearable and self-reported data remains a significant challenge due to intrinsic noise and complex, non-linear relationships within physiological metrics. To address this, we propose a Two-Tier Stacked Ensemble Model (TTSEM) specifically architected to enhance predictive robustness in noisy environments. The study utilizes a synthetic dataset of 20,000 health records, intentionally injected with 15% label noise to mirror real-world data inconsistencies. The ensemble’s primary tier leverages Random Forest, XGBoost, and CatBoost algorithms to generate probabilistic outputs. These are subsequently synthesized by a Logistic Regression meta-learner in the secondary tier. Empirical evaluations reveal that the TTSEM framework yields an accuracy of 85.48%, alongside a precision of 0.8574, a recall of 0.9203, and an F1-score of 0.8877. This exceptionally high recall minimizes false negatives, ensuring the reliable identification of fit individuals. Furthermore, the system is integrated into a Flask-based deployment architecture, facilitating scalable, real-time inferencing. Ultimately, these findings underscore the efficacy of stacked ensemble techniques in mitigating health data noise, proving highly suitable for modern health informatics applications.

title: Cross-Scenario Multi-Type Fault Diagnosis for Lithium-Ion Batteries via Deep Transfer Learning
authors: Xiaoke Li, Jingwen Wei, Ling Xie, Chunlin Chen, Guangzhong Dong
publish: IEEE Transactions on Transportation Electrification
abstract: Faults in lithium-ion batteries, such as low capacity (LowC) and micro short-circuits (MSC), can progressively deteriorate system reliability and may eventually trigger catastrophic safety incidents. Although existing diagnostic methods show promising performance in controlled laboratory settings, their reliance on artificially simulated data and their focus on single-scenario or single-fault conditions significantly limit their applicability in real-world deployments. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a lightweight neural network framework for multi-fault diagnosis with cross-scenario adaptability, enabling effective knowledge transfer from electric vehicles (EVs) to industrial and commercial energy storage systems (ICES). First, an effective feature extraction method, termed the 10-bin probability distribution matrix (10-BPDM), is introduced to compactly encode fault-relevant temporal–statistical characteristics. Then, a depthwise separable convolutional network integrated with a hybrid attention mechanism is designed to accurately detect and classify LowC and MSC faults. Extensive experiments conducted on real-world battery datasets demonstrate that the proposed method achieves accurate multi-fault diagnosis while maintaining strong cross-scenario transferability. In particular, by fine-tuning a model pre-trained on EV data using only a small number of target samples, a fault detection rate of 98.25% is achieved on the ICES dataset, confirming both the effectiveness and deployment feasibility of the proposed lightweight framework.

title: KEditVis: A Visual Analytics System for Knowledge Editing of Large Language Models
authors: Zhenning Chen, Hanbei Zhan, Yanwei Huang, Xin Wu, Dazhen Deng, Di Weng, Yingcai Wu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) demonstrate exceptional capabilities in factual question answering, yet they sometimes provide incorrect responses. To address this issue, knowledge editing techniques have emerged as effective methods for correcting factual information in LLMs. However, typical knowledge editing workflows struggle with identifying the optimal set of model layers for editing and rely on summary indicators that provide insufficient guidance. This lack of transparency hinders effective comparison and identification of optimal editing strategies. In this paper, we present KEditVis, a novel visual analytics system designed to assist users in gaining a deeper understanding of knowledge editing through interactive visualizations, improving editing outcomes, and discovering valuable insights for the future development of knowledge editing algorithms. With KEditVis, users can select appropriate layers as the editing target, explore the reasons behind ineffective edits, and perform more targeted and effective edits. Our evaluation, including usage scenarios, expert interviews, and a user study, validates the effectiveness and usability of the system.

title: Upper and Lower-Limb Motor Decoding for Adaptive and Generalized Neural Rehabilitation_supp1-3694007.pdf
authors: Daehee Seo
publish: N/A

title: A Dual-Modal Dual-Fusion Framework for UAV Classification Using Multistatic RCS Sequences
authors: Yaofei Yu, Bo Pang, Yuxia Ma, Chuansheng Lin, Haobin Yu
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: The rapid proliferation of Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) raises significant security concerns, necessitating reliable classification systems. Multistatic radar, which observes targets from multiple perspectives, provides superior classification capability over monostatic systems. To harness this advantage, this paper proposes a Dual-Modal Dual-Fusion (DMDF) framework for UAV classification using multistatic radar cross-section (RCS) sequences. The proposed DMDF framework processes two modalities of RCS data: a raw temporal branch and an image branch formed by superimposing two Gramian angular field (GAF) transformations. Local features are extracted from both branches using multi-scale deep separable convolution (DSC), while global dependencies are captured by a gated recurrent unit (GRU) layer in the temporal branch and a self-attention (SA) mechanism in the image branch. Features within the same branch are fused via concatenation. The features from both modalities are fed into classification modules to produce respective probability vectors, and the outputs of the two modalities are fused using Bayesian fusion based on optimal probability estimation. To evaluate our method, we simulated UAV flight trajectories with random perturbations and conducted experiments on a measured dataset containing RCS data from seven UAVs. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed DMDF framework achieves high classification accuracy under varying signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) conditions.

title: Data-efficient Cross-domain Few-shot Website Fingerprinting with Unsupervised Domain Adaptation
authors: Siyu Wang, Xiaoyan Hu, Guang Cheng, Ruidong Li, Hua Wu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Information Forensics and Security
abstract: Website fingerprinting (WF) attacks identify Torencrypted websites but struggle with cross-domain scenarios due to traffic distribution shifts. The existing few-shot WF attacks address the cross-domain problem with excessive auxiliary data, significantly reducing deployment efficiency. This work proposes UDA-WF, a data-efficient few-shot WF with Unsupervised Domain Adaptation (UDA). UDA-WF first pre-trains the feature extractor with limited auxiliary data in the source website domain. Then, it extracts the invariant feature space by computing the intersection of the source and target feature spaces through the unsupervised domain adaptation with the softmatch mechanism. Finally, UDA-WF fine-tunes the feature extractor and a single-layer perceptron to extract the discriminative unique feature space of the target website domain. We evaluate UDA-WF on our WF dataset collected over multiple months. UDA-WF significantly overcomes the cross-domain problem while reducing auxiliary data requirements by 95% and pre-training bootstrap time by 99% compared to the State-Of-The-Art (SOTA) methods. UDA-WF achieves an accuracy of 97.37% under the 20-shot setting in the closed-world scenario and outperforms SOTA methods. To further demonstrate the model’s adaptability to diverse real-world requirements, we validate it on the DF and Wang datasets, achieving accuracies exceeding 92% and 94%, respectively. Moreover, the results show that our UDA-WF is more resilient to concept drift and robust to WF defense.

title: Protecting Solid-State Transformers: Insights Into Mechanisms in Smart and Future Electrified Grids
authors: Kushan Lulbadda, Ruvini De Seram, Tarlochan Sidhu, Sheldon Williamson
publish: IEEE Industrial Electronics Magazine
abstract: Solid-state transformers (SSTs) represent a significant advancement in modern power systems, offering improved efficiency, adaptability, and compatibility with renewable energy sources, smart grids, and electric vehicle (EV) infrastructure. However, their unique design, including high-frequency operation and advanced semiconductor components, poses specific challenges that require effective protection mechanisms. This article investigates modern protection strategies compatible with SSTs, focusing on overvoltage and overcurrent protection, thermal management, and short circuit fault detection. The discussion also explores the integration of smart technologies, advancements in materials, and enhanced thermal performance as critical areas for future development. To support the discussion, a case study is presented, detailing the implementation of a solid-state protection system integrated with an SST, developed by the authors. Advanced protection strategies are highlighted with key challenges to enhance the reliability of SSTs in dynamic power systems. Additionally, the article explores future directions for improving SST protection mechanisms.

title: Generalized Discrete-Time Equivalent Circuit Modeling of Mutual Inductance
authors: Bilal Canol, Ali Bekir Yildiz
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems I: Regular Papers
abstract: This paper proposes a generalized, step-by-step companion-circuit formulation for mutual inductances with arbitrary numbers of windings and dot polarities. Starting from the continuous-time terminal relations, the method systematically converts coupled-inductor differential equations into a purely algebraic discrete-time equivalent network consisting of resistors, independent sources, and voltage-controlled current sources, enabling direct assembly in modified nodal analysis without transformer-equivalent conversions. Thereby, it provides a significant simplification in obtaining system equations and numerical implementation. This modeling framework offers an alternative method for simulating circuits containing double, triple, or multi-winding coupled inductive elements in discrete-time electromagnetic transient analysis programs and other circuit simulation environments. Although Backward Euler is used to demonstrate the derivation and to build a fully discrete-time model, the proposed modeling procedure is numerical-method agnostic, general and can be transferred to other integration schemes by replacing the underlying discretization of the terminal relations. Validation is performed on a three-winding coupled-inductor circuit with mixed R–L and C loads and on a switched-circuit case, both are benchmarked against circuit simulators under identical terminal conditions. Benchmark results indicate that the proposed method provides a significant computational cost advantage and very high numerical accuracy.

title: FocusED: An NLP-Driven Personalized and Affective-Aware Learning Framework for Neurodivergent Students
authors: C. Gayathri, Yedhu Krishnan. TV, S. Santhosh Kumar, K. Poovarasan, M. Sri Harish Vel
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Electronic Devices and Intelligent Systems (ICSEDIS)
abstract: Neurodivergent students, including learners with attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD), autism spectrum disorder (ASD), dyslexia, and anxiety, often experience difficulties when interacting with conventional digital learning platforms designed for neurotypical cognitive patterns. Most existing systems emphasize content delivery and assessment while providing limited personalization and minimal support for emotional well-being. To address these challenges, this paper proposes FocusED, a personalized learning framework that integrates natural language processing, multimodal behavioral modeling, and affect-aware adaptation. The system continuously collects learner interaction data to construct latent cognitive profiles that guide adaptive content sequencing and presentation. A neuro-symbolic NLP module simplifies complex instructional text and converts key concepts into visual representations to improve comprehension. In addition, an affective inference component identifies emotional states and provides supportive interventions. Experimental evaluation using learner interaction logs shows that FocusED achieves 96.51% learning outcome prediction accuracy, outperforming existing adaptive and deep learning tutoring systems while improving engagement, comprehension, and emotional stability.

title: AocSeg: Adaptive Obfuscated Contrast for Semi-supervised Semantic Segmentation Method for Unmanned Vehicles
authors: Fenglei Xu, Jinshan Pan, Zhongwei Shen, Fa Zhu, Mingwu Ren, Ming Gao, Witold Pedrycz, Athanasios V. Vasilakos
publish: IEEE Transactions on Consumer Electronics
abstract: Semantic segmentation techniques are crucial for surrounding perception in Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) and Unmanned Ground Vehicle (UGV) utilization. However, limited labeled datasets often lead to inadequate algorithm convergence and variability in training difficulty across different categories. This study introduces a data enhancement strategy called AocSeg to address these challenges. This method is a centroid alignmentbased data enhancement approach that optimizes the training process by adaptively generating additional reliable data. Specifically, it generates new training data by randomly selecting source and target image pairs from a labeled dataset and stitching these images at the pixel level. The AocSeg method comprises two key modules: Component Disturbance Module (CDM) and Instance Confounding Module (ICM). CDM assesses the correlation between predicted results and ground truth by applying feature perturbation techniques, thereby quantifying the training difficulty for each category. ICM then further analyzes the challenging categories identified by CDM by computing pixel-level confusion memory to discern the most confusing categories among them. Extensive experiments are conducted on the Cityscapes and Pascal VOC dataset to validate the effectiveness of AocSeg. Additionally, we constructed a Bird’s Eye View (BEV) dataset tailored to real UAV and UGV scenarios, further validating the practical application and effectiveness of the proposed method. Our code is available at https://github.com/pjs1/AocSeg.

title: Context-Aware Filtering of PolSAR Data Based on Quaternion Wavelet Shrinkage
authors: Yuta Otsuka, Ryo Natsuaki, Akira Hirose
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Polarimetric synthetic aperture radar (PolSAR) is widely used for land cover classification. One of its major problems is the effect of speckle. Although numerous methods have been developed to suppress speckle noise, it is difficult to classify complex land cover types composed of multiple different scattering mechanisms by pixel-wise classification with existing filtering methods. In this paper, we propose context-aware PolSAR filters based on quaternion wavelet shrinkage to solve this problem. We first represent logarithmic-transformed Stokes vectors (log Stokes vectors) in a quaternion form. Next, we apply the quaternion wavelet transform to the quaternion images of log Stokes vectors. Then, we perform filtering in the quaternion wavelet domain by shrinking small wavelet coefficients. Log Stokes vectors in areas with highly random polarimetric information, i.e., areas where the polarization state varies from pixel to pixel, are strongly filtered, while those in homogeneous areas are weakly filtered or unfiltered. Therefore, after the inverse transform, filtered log Stokes vectors incorporate spatial-context information as the degree of polarization (DoP). This results in accurate classification of complex land cover types without using contextual classifiers or a large number of texture features. Experimental results show that the proposed filters are more suitable than conventional filters for the preprocessing of pixel-wise land cover classification.

title: An IoT-Driven Intelligent Drainage Safety System using Federated Machine Learning for Real-Time Gas and Blockage Prediction
authors: Navaneetha Krishnan M, C.N.Vanitha, Gokul Nath S, Dhanush G, PrasanthT, Bharaniraj M
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Electronic Devices and Intelligent Systems (ICSEDIS)
abstract: Urban drainage systems often experience hazardous gas accumulation and physical blockages that threaten maintenance workers and public safety. Conventional drainage monitoring methods rely on periodic inspections and centralized data processing, which suffer from delayed response, communication overhead, and limited scalability. To address these challenges, this study proposes an IoT-driven intelligent drainage safety system that integrates distributed sensing with federated machine learning for real-time gas leakage and blockage prediction. Multiple low-power IoT sensor nodes continuously monitor environmental parameters such as methane concentration, hydrogen sulfide levels, water flow rate, and obstruction indicators. Edge computing devices locally process sensor data and train predictive models, while federated learning enables collaborative model updates without sharing raw data, thereby preserving privacy and reducing network bandwidth usage. Experimental evaluation demonstrates that the proposed federated learning approach achieves improved prediction performance with an accuracy of 93.6%, outperforming both rule-based and centralized machine learning models. Furthermore, edge-based processing significantly reduces response latency and accelerates alert generation. The proposed framework provides a scalable, privacy-preserving, and efficient solution for intelligent drainage monitoring, contributing to safer urban infrastructure and smart city development.

title: Auditing Hallucination in Lightweight Vision-Language Models for Radiology Captioning
authors: Ziwei Wang, Haoyun Zhang, Chen Xie, Di Zhu, Zihan Wei
publish: 2026 International Conference on Generative Artificial Intelligence and Information Security (GAIIS)
abstract: Lightweight vision-language models (VLMs) are attractive for medical image captioning because they run on commodity hardware, but their reliability on radiology images remains poorly characterized. We audited two widely used open-source lightweight captioning models, BLIP-Base and ViT-GPT2, on 991 real radiology images collected from the ROCO dataset. Each image was evaluated in four conditions (clean, Gaussian noise, center crop, and low contrast), yielding 7,928 captions. We assessed a stratified sample of 400 captions with an automated semantic judge and computed exact medical-term string metrics for the full set of 7,928 outputs. Both models hallucinated on every judged caption across every condition. At the model level, hallucination was observed in 200/200 judged outputs for BLIP-Base and 200/200 for ViT-GPT2 (95% exact confidence interval 98.2–100.0 for both). Omission was universal in both models, while fabrication was markedly higher for ViT-GPT2 than for BLIP-Base (97.0% versus 68.0%). BLIP-Base retained limited modality and anatomy recognition (29.5% and 23.5%, respectively), whereas ViT-GPT2 achieved 0% on both endpoints. Input perturbations did not materially worsen performance because failure was already saturated on clean images. In contrast, string matching produced misleadingly favorable scores for ViT-GPT2 through incidental lexical overlap with the reference captions. These results show that lightweight general-purpose VLMs can appear operationally convenient while remaining fundamentally unsuitable for radiology captioning without domain-specific adaptation and semantically grounded evaluation.

title: Applications of Intelligent Image Recognition Technology in Modern Security Inspection and the Evolution of Screening Equipment
authors: Xiang Shen, Guangming Yang
publish: 2026 International Conference on Generative Artificial Intelligence and Information Security (GAIIS)
abstract: With the increasing complexity of public security risks, traditional security inspection systems that rely on manual image interpretation are gradually being replaced by proactive intelligent paradigms driven by deep learning, computer vision, and generative artificial intelligence. This paper provides a systematic review of the intelligent evolution of modern security screening equipment. It examines the technological foundations of intelligent image recognition, including convolutional neural networks, three-dimensional deep learning, generative models, and vision-language models, and analyzes their roles in baggage screening and human body inspection. In baggage screening, X-ray computed tomography systems, supported by volumetric reconstruction, three-dimensional convolutional neural networks, and automatic explosive detection algorithms, have significantly improved the capability to resolve object overlap and occlusion, thus promoting the transition from operator-assisted interpretation to machine-dominant decision support. In human body inspection, millimeter-wave imaging systems combined with automatic target recognition and privacy-preserving display mechanisms have enabled accurate detection of concealed nonmetallic threats while maintaining human-centered design. The paper further summarizes key technical challenges, including small object detection, unknown threat adaptation, limited edge computing resources, and data scarcity, and discusses future directions such as multimodal sensor fusion, non-intrusive screening, continual model evolution, and holistic intelligent security ecosystems. In particular, this review is organized along the technological evolution path of modern security inspection and highlights the applicability boundaries of representative technologies in terms of accuracy, speed, cost, and deployment role.

title: A Dual-Stage Convolutional Framework for Pancreatic Tumor Detection and Classification on Pancreas CT Images
authors: Dhishya E, Anandababu P
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Electronic Devices and Intelligent Systems (ICSEDIS)
abstract: Examination of pancreatic tumors according to the computed tomography (CT) images has been a challenging task due to the low tissue contrast, high variability in anatomy, and small morphological difference between normal and pathological regions. This paper will present a new two-stage convolutional network capable of further improving the performance of detection and classification and have an economical cost to implement in the clinic. The first stage involves the segmentation of pancreatic specific regions by using a lightweight encoder decoder and features contrast sensitive to extraction with boundary preserving convolutional unit blocks. The second processing is the guided region-of-interest classifier stage that involves a small convolutional architecture to detect the normal tissue and tumor-affected region by utilizing the fine-tuned segmentation mask. The proposed model is evaluated using the conventional measures, which are more sensitive and specific than the traditional single-stage CNN baselines. It also has a modular design that enables it to be straightforwardly incorporated into downstream diagnostic pipelines so that it can be extended in the future to incorporate transformer-based fusion or generative augmentation frameworks. Overall, the system has provided a strong and justifiable approach of early detection of pancreatal tumors.

title: EnsembleShield-DM: Interpretable, Shift-Robust Ensemble Learning for Type 2 Diabetes Prediction Across Multi-Regional Cohorts
authors: Aylar Seyedi, Mohammadreza Pournameti, Mahdieh Mosavi
publish: 2026 Fourth International Conference on Distributed Computing and High Performance Computing (DCHPC)
abstract: Early identification of diabetes is essential to prevent serious complications and reduce healthcare costs. Many existing predictive models perform well only on limited regional datasets and lose accuracy when applied to new populations. This study presents a scalable and interpretable ensemble learning framework for diabetes prediction using clinical and demographic variables collected from two regional datasets and their merged cohort under a unified structure. The proposed pipeline applies class-aware imputation, data standardization, and synthetic minority oversampling to correct missing information and class imbalance. Three base classifiers, Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, and Logistic Regression, are combined through soft voting, while a confidence-based decision rule improves reliability when model uncertainty is high. The interpretability of the system is evaluated with SHAP values, which clarify the influence of individual features. Experimental results show an average area under the receiver operating characteristic curve above 0.95 with balanced precision and recall across the evaluated datasets. The proposed framework demonstrates stable cross-dataset performance, calibration-aware risk estimation, interpretability, and reproducibility within the studied cohorts, providing a solid foundation for future multi-center or federated investigations in diabetes prediction.

title: Application and Evaluation of a Multi-Model Assisted Human-Review Workflow for Disaster Image Annotation
authors: Renjie Cao, Dezhi Wang, Wei Li, Hang Zheng, Jun Zhang
publish: 2026 International Conference on Generative Artificial Intelligence and Information Security (GAIIS)
abstract: Disaster image annotation is time-consuming and demands considerable domain expertise from annotators. This paper presents a multi-model assisted human-review workflow for disaster image annotation. The workflow comprises three stages: dual-model independent annotation, consistency checking, and human review. Two multimodal vision-language models, Qwen3.5-Plus and GLM-4.6V, are invoked via API to independently perform bounding-box annotation and semantic description on the same image. The annotation results from the two models are then subjected to consistency checking by task type (IoU comparison for bounding boxes; DeepSeek-V3.2 comparison for semantic descriptions), and all results are presented to annotators as review references. Annotators may modify any annotation content, and the final results are submitted only after human confirmation. Using 600 wildfire images and 600 earthquake disaster images as experimental data, a controlled experiment was conducted comparing purely manual annotation (main-experiment Group A) with the proposed workflow (main-experiment Group B). Results show that the proposed workflow reduced annotation time by 72% in the wildfire scenario and 65% in the earthquake scenario compared to the purely manual group, while annotation accuracy differences between the two groups remained within 4 percentage points. This paper also identifies two typical scenarios where model assistance is limited.

title: Blockchain-Enabled Secure Transaction Architecture for Fraud Detection and Risk Management in Financial Institutions
authors: Jacob Davis K, A. Thilagaraj
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Electronic Devices and Intelligent Systems (ICSEDIS)
abstract: The Fraud protection and risk management are still major concerns for financial institutions in highly digitized settings. This paper presents a blockchain-based secure transaction system that utilizes HyperDimensional Computing (HDC) and blockchain technology to provide enhanced institutional robustness against fraudulent transactions. The blockchain technology enables the creation of immutable and transparent records that promote trust and accountability, while the HDC technology provides unparalleled capabilities in anomaly detection for complex high-dimensional financial data. The proposed system takes advantage of the best of both worlds and provides an excellent platform for real-time fraud detection and risk analysis. The experimental result shows an accuracy of 98%, thus confirming the efficacy of the proposed approach in achieving a balance between security, scalability, and efficiency. This research aims to help in the development of intelligent financial infrastructure using computational intelligence and trust in a decentralized manner, which will serve as a way forward for secure and trustworthy digital transactions. However, apart from the benefits of this research, the proposed architecture is also aimed at being deployable in real-world financial systems. By incorporating HyperDimensional Computing into the blockchain transaction process, the system is able to adapt to the latest patterns of fraud without having to extensively retrain. The decentralized system also ensures that the risk assessments are transparent and verifiable. More importantly, the system is able to adapt to the latest regulations on computational efficiency.

title: A New Approach for Coupling Absorbers to Magnetic Microcalorimeters
authors: Nathan Hines, Luke Emmert, Inwook Kim, Chang Lee, Tashi Parsons-Davis, Josh Wimpenny, S. T. P. Boyd, Geon-Bo Kim
publish: IEEE Transactions on Applied Superconductivity
abstract: Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory is developing magnetic microcalorimeter-based decay energy spectrometry for nuclear materials analysis. Our standard technique uses gold wire bonds to thermally connect large, external absorbers to microcalorimeter sensors, which limits signal rise time and thus the maximum measurable activity. To address these limitations, we have developed a new absorber sensor direct coupling approach. We find that the direct-coupling approach achieves an order of magnitude reduction in signal rise time as compared to our standard method. We have also demonstrated that the direct-coupling technique enables accurate isotopic analysis, with results in agreement with those obtained by mass spectrometry. This report describes standard and direct coupling DES measurement techniques and presents measurement results, including a comparison of signal rise time, signal amplitude, and isotopic analysis between the two configurations.

title: DiCLIP: Diffusion Model Enhances CLIP&#x2019;s Dense Knowledge for Weakly Supervised Semantic Segmentation_supp1-3692055.pdf
authors: Zhiwei Yang
publish: N/A
abstract: Weakly Supervised Semantic Segmentation (WSSS) with image-level labels typically leverages Class Activation Maps (CAMs) to achieve pixel-level predictions. Recently, Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training (CLIP) has been introduced to generate CAMs in WSSS. However, previous WSSS methods solely adopt CLIP’s vision-language paired property for dense localization, neglecting its inherently limited dense knowledge across both visual and text modalities, which renders CAM generation suboptimal. In this work, we propose DiCLIP, a novel WSSS framework that leverages the generative diffusion model to enhance CLIP’s dense knowledge across two modalities. Specifically, Visual Correlation Enhancement (VCE) and Text Semantic Augmentation (TSA) modules are proposed for dense prediction enhancement. To improve the spatial awareness of visual features, our VCE module utilizes diffusion’s reliable spatial consistency to mitigate the over-smoothing issue in CLIP’s attention. It designs the Attention Clustering Refinement (ACR) module to reliably extract diverse correlation maps from the diffusion model. The correlation maps act as a diversity bias for CLIP’s self-attention, recursively pushing its visual features towards a more discriminative dense distribution. To augment the semantics of text embeddings, our TSA module argues that a single text modality is insufficient to encompass the variability of visual categories. Thus, we leverage diffusion’s generative power to maintain a dynamic key-value cache model, shifting CAM generation from a patch-text matching mechanism to a novel visual knowledge retrieval paradigm.With these enhancements, DiCLIP not only outperforms state-of-the-art methods on PASCAL VOC and MS COCO but also significantly reduces training costs. Code will be publicly available at https://github.com/zwyang6/DiCLIP.

title: A Novel Deep Learning Approaches for Brain Stroke Detection System on CT Images
authors: Anitha Thangasamy, Raghi K R, Kommi Bharath, Kondragunta Bobby
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Electronic Devices and Intelligent Systems (ICSEDIS)
abstract: Brain stroke has been a leading cause of death and irreversible neurological disabilities in the world, and thus the diagnosis of neurological disorders in the early stages and correctly will ensure successful clinical management. The proposed algorithmic system of stroke-impacted region identification and segmentation in the CT scan of the brain incorporates the deep learning and machine learning approaches. The pretrained convolutional neural network models (ResNet18 and EfficientNetB0) were used to determine whether the CT scans were stroke or non-stroke. The classical machine learning classifiers and novel deep learning approaches are used to further improve the diagnostic. The experiment results show that the hemorrhagic stroke is accurately determined. The proposed deep learning models with traditional machine learning methods to help clinicians to brain stroke earlier.

title: Transient Detection on EAF Current Signals Sampled at Low Rates via Statistical Analysis
authors: Erhan Sezgin, Ebrahim Balouji, Özgül Salor
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industry Applications
abstract: Electric Arc Furnaces (EAFs) introduce stochastic current transients that degrade electrode life and power quality. Detecting such events is challenging when high-frequency components are attenuated by the anti-aliasing filters of standard monitoring devices. This paper proposes a fast, deterministic, and training-free transient detector operating on EAF currents sampled at low rates compared to transients. The method forms half-cycle RMS features, extracts a residual via a light Savitzky–Golay trend estimate, and applies numerical differentiation followed by a sliding-window Z-score test with hysteresis logic to robustly label transients. Performance is evaluated on both synthetic signals and field measurements, and is benchmarked against a robust LSTM autoencoder (LSTM-AE) reconstruction-based detector enhanced with robust normalization, training cleaning, and adaptive thresholding. In the evaluated cold-start setting, the proposed approach significantly outperforms the deep learning baseline. While the LSTM-AE suffers from severe event fragmentation and degrades to an F1-score of 0.13 on actual EAF measurements, the proposed method maintains a robust F1-score of 0.68. Furthermore, it operates with substantially lower computational overhead and no training requirement, making it highly suitable for low-latency industrial monitoring in EAF installations.
