title: Residual Deformable Attention Framework with Detection Transformer for Early and Accurate Skin Cancer Detection from Dermoscopic Images
authors: Karamala Naveen, M. Humera Khanam
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Electronic Devices and Intelligent Systems (ICSEDIS)
abstract: Melanoma is an exceptionally prevalent and deadly type of skin cancer that affects people all over the world. Early detection significantly increases melanoma patients' chances of recovery. Deep learning techniques are currently becoming more and more popular for helping in melanoma early detection. Even if image classifiers perform well, depending only on them damages the application's credibility and makes it hard to suggest the reason behind the predictions. To address this issue, this study proposes an advanced deep structured learning-based model for accurate skin cancer detection. In the initial phase, the input images are gathered from benchmark databases. These gathered images undergo an image pre-processing phase where Detection Transformer and Bilateral Filtering are applied to reduce noise while preserving important edges of the skin lesion. The pre-processed images are leveraged by the Residual Deformable Detection Transformer Network (RD-DeTNet) for the detection of skin cancer. Finally, the proposed RD-DeTNet offers the skin cancer detection outcome. The performance of the proposed system is verified using various performance metrics, demonstrating its superiority compared to traditional detection models. Therefore, it substantially enhanced clinical decision support and overall performance in computerized skin cancer analysis by enabling the early detection of malignant patterns and increasing diagnostic consistency.

title: Enhancing Network Security with Software-Defined Networking
authors: J.Jenifer, Anita Antwiwaa, Sogy K Idicula, Gayathiri Devi S, Gomathi N.
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Electronic Devices and Intelligent Systems (ICSEDIS)
abstract: With enterprises growing rapidly through new cloud technologies, they are exposed to new sophisticated cyber threats that traditional security solutions are not able to manage effectively. In addition to insufficient management capabilities, existing, traditional distributed architectures historically suffer from limited real-time, centralized visibility; inflexible controls; and slow policy implementation processes that create challenges to defending against modern networks. The paper presents an innovative, intelligent Software Defined Networking (SDN)-enabled security framework, which integrates centralized programmability, machine learning-based intrusion detection and automated mitigation to facilitate modern cyber defense. This architecture incorporates the division of control and data into separate planes, provides a global view of the network, enables the ability to dynamically manage traffic flows within the network, and allows consistent enforcement of security policies across multiple systems in the network, enabling the ability to secure today’s operating environments. The framework uses a customized hybrid approach to detect and identify both known and unknown threats; that is, the framework uses both signature-based and supervised learning-based filtering. To validate the architecture, it was tested with standard benchmark intrusion data sets in realistic laboratory conditions, while the performance evaluation of the architecture shows that it is highly successful in detecting potential indicator of attacks, providing a good level of precision and recall, producing a low false positive rate, requiring a minimal amount of additional resources to implement automated responses to network traffic, while minimizing the network overhead of automated responses. Therefore, the results support the conclusion that combining an intelligent analytical capability with SDN can meet the cybersecurity requirements of large enterprise environments and networks through a dependable scalable, adaptable and efficient solution.

title: TcNc: Token comparison and noise correction for Weakly-Supervised Semantic Segmentation
authors: Weijie Su
publish: 2026 International Conference on Generative Artificial Intelligence and Information Security (GAIIS)
abstract: Weakly supervised semantic segmentation methods utilizing image-level labels have garnered increasing attention due to their significant reduction in annotation costs and broader applicability. CNN-based weakly supervised semantic segmentation methods have limitations, as CNNs struggle to capture sufficient global information. While the emergence of Transformer networks effectively addresses this issue, Vision Transformer (VIT) networks often overlook the distinction between global and local image features. This leads to excessive smoothing and poor edge detail recognition in target regions. We propose the Token Comparison and Noise Correction (TcNc) weakly supervised semantic segmentation method. This approach employs a Token Comparison Module (TCM) to address VIT's over-smoothing issue and utilizes a Class Comparison Module (CCM) to encourage CAM activation across more object regions. Additionally, a Noise Correction Module (NCM) is designed to evaluate the quality of initial pseudo labels, then outputs a new pseudo label to correct weak labels into more reliable ones, improving pseudo label noise through an online approach. Experiments on the PASCAL VOC 2012 dataset demonstrate that the proposed TcNc significantly outperforms other single-stage and multi-stage methods.

title: Real-Time Navigation Assistant for Visually Impaired People using YOLOv8 and Voice Alerts
authors: K.Parvathi, Thota Kesava Rao, P Srinivasa Rao, S. Gopinath
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Electronic Devices and Intelligent Systems (ICSEDIS)
abstract: The lack of situational awareness and slow perception of the obstacles around leads to safe navigation of visually impaired people in dynamic outdoor and indoor environments as a primary challenge. Despite the recent assistive navigation systems that use computer vision and audio feedback, most of the current methods have high computational latency, low detection accuracy, and low real-time functioning in complex settings. To overcome these weaknesses, the current research paper suggests a real-time navigation helper, which combines visual perception with deep learning and adaptive voice navigation. This proposed system provides a framework called the YOLOv8 object detection that is used to detect and localize the obstacles that are of interest to navigation like pedestrians, vehicles, exits, stairsteps, and street fixtures using live camera streams. Items found are compared in terms of spatial relevance and distance to the navigation route of the user, and context-based voice notifications are created to give appropriate direction. The system is lightweight and can be deployed on edge devices, allowing the system to act as a continuous operating system that consumes little computational load. Benchmark dataset and real-time camera stream experimental analysis reveal high detection ability with a mean average precision (mAP) of 0.5) of 88.6, precision of 90.2, and recall of 86.9. The system has an average of 52 frames per second and an end-to-end of nearly 19 ms. All these findings suggest that the presented framework can be successfully used to increase the awareness of obstacles and enhance independent mobility and the safety of the visually impaired users.

title: Edge-Aware Unified Retinex Architecture for Adaptive Low-Light Enhancement of Images and Videos Across Diverse Domains in Real-Time
authors: Lakkakula Sai Raveena Sowgandhika, Madem Sanjana Sarvani, A. Yovan Felix
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Electronic Devices and Intelligent Systems (ICSEDIS)
abstract: The images and videos which are captured in low-light conditions or dark environments will lead to poor visibility, quality, low contrast, amplified noise in the applications namely Surveillance, Autonomous Vehicles, Medical Imaging, Mobile Photography. The existing systems either fails in training the paired datasets, or the temporal consistency of videos. This work presents a real-time hybrid unified architecture that combines the classical image processing techniques and advanced deep learning model i.e., Multi-Scale Retinex (MSR) for enhancing the uneven lighting conditions, Multi-Scale Retinex Color Restoration for restoring the color (MSRCR), Zero-Reference Deep Curve Estimation (Zero-DCE++) for unsupervised adaptive refinement requiring no paired training data. The noise suppression stage which reduces the unwanted noise while preserving the fine structure details and boundaries. For video inputs, it undergoes temporal consistency which avoids the flickering between the frames and gains 30 frames per second. Performance evaluation across various applications yields Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR) of 31.0db and Structural Similarity Index of Measure (SSIM) of 0.93, which confirms the effectiveness of the system. The lightweight architecture have the ability to enhance the images and videos while preserving the natural colors across various domains in real-world deployable environments.

title: NAVIGATE: Multi-Agent Graph-Based Vulnerability Detection with Adversarial Severity Assessment
authors: Yang Guo
publish: 2026 International Conference on Generative Artificial Intelligence and Information Security (GAIIS)
abstract: Automated vulnerability severity assessment is critical for prioritizing security remediation efforts, yet existing approaches face significant challenges. Traditional text-based methods rely solely on vulnerability descriptions, lacking the technical depth present in source code. Meanwhile, Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown promise in code understanding but struggle with long input sequences and lack structured semantic information, leading to unreliable severity predictions. This paper presents NAVIGATE, a multi-agent graph-centric framework that synergizes Code Property Graphs (CPGs) with LLM-powered reasoning for automated vulnerability severity assessment. A key innovation is the deployment of an autonomous ReAct (Reasoning + Acting) agent with a documentation subagent for dynamic query generation, enabling the discovery of complex vulnerability patterns without relying on predefined static rules. Another core contribution is the adversarial multi-agent debate mechanism using AG2 (AutoGen 2), where Security Analyst, Red Team Expert, Blue Team Expert, and Severity Judge agents engage in structured deliberation to achieve consensus-based severity ratings. Our approach employs a multi-stage pipeline comprising: (1) CPG-based code representation, (2) dual-agent vulnerability discovery with context compression, (3) dataflow-guided slicing with Tree-sitter-based syntactic reconstruction, (4) LLM-based path filtering with Top-K selection, and (5) adversarial debate for CVSS v3-aligned severity classification. Experiments on a benchmark of 594 CVEs in C/C++ demonstrate that NAVIGATE achieves a severity classification accuracy of 72.73%, F1-score of 70.0%, and MCC of 0.544. Compared to the SVACL baseline [1] (63.47% accuracy), our dataflow-guided slicing approach—which uses Joern’s inter-procedural reachableByFlows analysis to extract vulnerability-relevant code paths—improves accuracy by 9.26 percentage points.

title: PDO-SFCM: Prediction-Driven Orchestration for SFC Migration in SAGIN via Fine-Tuned Large Time-Series Model and DRL
authors: Jiang Mo, Ke Zhao, Limei Peng, Hsiao-Chun Wu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network and Service Management
abstract: Space-air-ground integrated networks (SAGINs) have emerged as an appealing enabling technology for the next-generation ubiquitous connectivity. By extending terrestrial networks with aerial and space platforms, SAGIN can provide seamless coverage and flexible resource-access across various altitudes. However, dynamic link conditions, intermittent connectivity, and heterogeneous latency constraints would often introduce serious challenges to the service function chain (SFC) migration and orchestration. In this work, we introduce a novel PDO-SFCM (prediction-driven orchestration for SFC migration) approach, which utilizes a fine-tuned large time-series model (LTM) for network status prediction and a deep reinforcement learning (DRL) module for proactive SFC migration in SAGINs. In detail, the fine-tuned LTM predicts multi-horizon estimates of SFC arrivals and per virtual network function (per-VNF) resource demands, which will form the observation space of the DRL agent. The DRL module thus schedules appropriate migration actions on the cost-augmented time-expanded graph (C-eTEG), which can satisfy the feasibility subject to the bandwidth, buffering, and precedence constraints. Extensive simulation results demonstrate that our proposed new PDO-SFCM scheme consistently greatly improves the acceptance rate, reduces the end-to-end delay, and lowers the migration cost in comparison with DRL baselines under different prediction settings. Our proposed new scheme can significantly leverage the SAGIN performance by the devised foundation-level time-series prediction and learning-based orchestration mechanisms.

title: Magnetic Field Reconstruction and Internal Current Inversion of GIS via Physics-Informed Deep Operator Network
authors: Xiangyu Guan, Xiaokun Chen, Hang Zhang, Chaoqun Xu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Magnetics
abstract: With multi-contact element parallel design, current distribution is closely related with assembly status of plug-in connector used in gas insulated switchgear (GIS). This work designs a physics-informed deep operator network (PI-DeepONet) framework to retrieve the internal current distribution among contact elements from reinforcement learning (RL) optimized sparse outside tank surface magnetic sensor array. The Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) based RL algorithm is firstly adopted to confirm optimal tank surface magnetic sensor layout. The PI-DeepONet consists of two parallel sub-networks, the branch network is employed to encode feature magnetic field and current vector and from magnetic sensor array observed data. Meanwhile, the trunk network to encodes space-time coordinates, efficiently mapping measurements to field solutions through operator learning. The governing partial differential equations for electromagnetic fields are embedded as physics loss terms to ensure physical consistency, while discrepancies between reconstructed and measured tank surface magnetic fields, along with total operational current differences, are incorporated as data loss terms to maintain measurement fidelity. Effectiveness of proposed method is compared with finite element analysis (FEA) and physical experiments, results show that the proposed approach achieves a magnetic field reconstruction maximum absolute error of 0.33 mT using 7 optimized outside magnetic sensors. With 28 times faster prediction speed compared to conventional simulations, while maintaining a current inversion maximum absolute error of 0.55 A under background noise conditions. Proposed technique can be implemented on magnetic-based monitor systems, thus enabling accurate internal contact fault diagnosis of GIS.

title: How Software Engineers Engage with AI: A Pragmatic Workflow
authors: Vəhid Gəruslu, Zafar Jafarov, Aytan Mövsümova, Atif Namazov
publish: IEEE Software
abstract: Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools such as GitHub Copilot and ChatGPT are increasingly used in software engineering (SE) for tasks such as code, test, and documentation generation. However, engineers often face uncertainty about when to trust, refine, or discard AI-generated artifacts. We present a pragmatic workflow, complemented by a four-quadrant decision model, that formalizes how developers iteratively prompt, inspect, refine, and, when needed, fall back to manual work. The workflow and decision model were derived from a grey literature review and field observations across three industrial settings in Türkiye and Azerbaijan. Two realworld scenarios demonstrate the workflow’s practical value, showing how engineers navigate key decision points when using AI. Our approach offers lightweight, semi-formalization of the decision-making process engineers already follow – implicitly or explicitly – when engaging with AI tools in everyday SE tasks, supporting more deliberate and quality-aware use of AI.".

title: RAG-Augmented Explainable Early Warning System for Acute Kidney Injury in the ICU: A KDIGO-Aligned XGBoost Pipeline with SHAP-Driven Evidence Retrieval on MIMIC-IV
authors: Kerem Gencer
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Acute kidney injury (AKI) affects up to 50% of critically ill patients and is independently associated with increased in-hospital mortality. Early, accurate identification of at-risk patients is critical yet remains challenging due to the heterogeneous presentation and multifactorial etiology of AKI in the intensive care unit (ICU). This paper presents AKI-PREDICT, a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG)-augmented clinical decision support framework that integrates gradient-boosted machine learning with SHAP-based explainability and biomedical evidence retrieval for early AKI prediction. The system is trained and evaluated on MIMIC-IV v3.1, a large real-world ICU database comprising 29,454 adult patients after KDIGO-aligned cohort construction. A strict temporal design confines all predictive features to the first 24 hours of ICU admission, with the AKI outcome window defined as hours 24–72, ensuring complete elimination of data leakage. The primary XGBoost model achieves an AUROC of 0.806 and AUPRC of 0.781 on the held-out test set, outperforming LightGBM (AUROC 0.804) and Random Forest (AUROC 0.786). A secondary multi-class XGBoost model performs KDIGO staging (Stage 0–3), achieving 68.4% overall accuracy. SHAP analysis identifies urine output rate (mL/kg/h, 0–24h), maximum serum creatinine, patient weight, and mechanical ventilation as the principal predictors of AKI onset. An ablation study demonstrates that urine output features contribute the largest single-group performance gain (ΔAUROC = 0.033), followed by laboratory values (ΔAUROC = 0.022). The RAG component retrieves semantically relevant excerpts from the KDIGO 2012 Clinical Practice Guideline and PubMed literature using SHAP-driven query construction and BioBERT-based embeddings indexed with FAISS, generating patient-specific, evidence-grounded clinical reports. Subgroup analysis confirms consistent discriminative performance across CKD, sepsis, vasopressor exposure, and age strata. AKI-PREDICT provides a fully reproducible, open-source pipeline linking prediction, explanation, and guideline-grounded evidence in a single workflow.

title: Priority Aware Q-Learning for Multi Objective Task Scheduling in Cloud Computing
authors: Harini Kota, S Sudheer Mangalampalli, Pillareddy Vamsheedhar Reddy
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Electronic Devices and Intelligent Systems (ICSEDIS)
abstract: In cloud computing, effective task scheduling is essential for enhancing system performance and reduce operating costs when workloads are dynamic. Our research aim is to improve the task-to-VM allocation choices by proposing a Priority-Aware Q-Learning based scheduling that includes task priority into the reinforcement learning. The proposed work uses a action-selection strategy to guarantee the stable and consistent scheduling behavior, in contrast to traditional stochastic reinforcement learning techniques. The extensive simulations are performed in SimPy using Google Cloud Jobs (GoCJ) to evaluate the model. When compared to the ACO and Q-Learning algorithms, experimental results show the improvement on proposed algorithm for makespan with 42.78% and 18.14%, Average waiting time with 36.88% and 16.42%, Average response time with 41.9% and 33.71% and cost with 33.87% and 17.18%. These improvements demonstrate that priority integration and effective workload distribution reduce delays as well as operational costs.

title: U-Sleep-RBD-KAN: Joint Sleep Staging and REM Behavior Disorder Detection with Interpretable Nonlinear Learning
authors: Ghadeer Yousef, S. Metilda Florence, Ali Mahmoud Mayya
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Rapid Eye Movement Behavior Disorder (RBD) is a clinically significant sleep disorder that is often associated with neurodegenerative conditions, making early and reliable detection important in sleep medicine. Similarly, accurate sleep stage classification is essential for understanding sleep architecture and supporting clinical decision-making. However, manual scoring of polysomnography (PSG) recordings is time-consuming and subject to inter-scorer variability, which limits scalability and consistency in practical applications. This paper proposes a unified deep learning framework, U-Sleep-RBD-KAN, for simultaneous sleep stage classification and RBD detection using multimodal PSG signals. The model integrates a U-shaped fully convolutional temporal network for hierarchical feature extraction, a REM-aware attention mechanism to emphasize physiologically relevant REM-related segments, and a Kolmogorov–Arnold Network (KAN) layer for structured nonlinear prediction. The framework is trained end-to-end on the Sleep-EDF Expanded dataset from PhysioNet, using EEG, EOG, and EMG signals segmented into 30-second epochs without manual feature engineering. Experimental results show that the proposed method achieves a sleep stage classification accuracy of 96.4% with a macro F1-score of 0.95, while subject-level RBD detection achieves an accuracy of 97.1%, a macro F1-score of 0.97, and an AUC of 0.992. These results indicate consistent performance across both tasks under a unified evaluation setup. Overall, the proposed framework demonstrates the effectiveness of combining multi-scale temporal modeling, REM-focused attention, and structured nonlinear learning for automated sleep analysis.

title: Brain-Controlled Target Speech Extraction Based on Disentanglement and Progressive Dual-Modal Fusion
authors: Xilei Ling, Haixian Wang
publish: 2026 International Conference on Generative Artificial Intelligence and Information Security (GAIIS)
abstract: Target speech extraction in cocktail-party environments remains challenging, especially when acoustic mixtures are highly overlapped and conventional speaker cues are unreliable. Inspired by the staged neural mechanism of auditory attention, we propose DPFNet, a brain-controlled target speech extraction framework based on disentanglement and progressive dual-modal fusion. DPFNet decomposes electroencephalogram (EEG) auditory attention cues into a static component for stable target-related prior and a dynamic component for time-varying tracking. Built on a SepFormer-style backbone, the model introduces a static auditory attention modulation separator to inject stable EEG priors into dual-path speech separation through adaptive modulation, and a dynamic auditory attention adaptive calibration module to further refine continuous acoustic representations using cross-modal interaction and temporal gating. In this way, DPFNet progressively integrates static identity-related information and dynamic auditory attention cues, yielding a neurophysiologically informed framework for brain-guided target speech extraction.

title: Federated Transformer–CNN Hybrid Framework for Privacy-Aware Multi-Hospital Disease Risk Prediction
authors: Sanjay Gandhi Gundabatini, B Durga Sravani, Y.Sherly Priscilla, Bunga Revathi, D Ratnagiri, Madhava Rao Maganti
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Electronic Devices and Intelligent Systems (ICSEDIS)
abstract: The digitization of multi-hospital health information has facilitated predictive disease risk modeling while also presenting problems about patient privacy, regulatory compliance, and limited cross-institutional data exchange. Traditional centralized learning approaches necessitate the transmission of sensitive patient data to distant servers, which is sometimes unfeasible in actual clinical settings. This article presents a privacy-preserving federated hybrid learning framework that combines Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) with Transformer-based contextual modeling for illness risk prediction in dispersed hospitals. The proposed architecture employs CNN encoders to extract spatial characteristics from medical pictures and structured patient records, whereas Transformer layers identify long-range correlations among temporal signals and demographic variables. Each participating hospital locally trains the model, sharing only encrypted model updates with a central coordinator for aggregation, thereby ensuring that raw patient data remains inside institutional confines. Experimental results indicate exceptional performance, attaining 96% accuracy, 93% precision, 95% recall, 94% F1-score, and 0.97 AUC, surpassing CNN-only, Transformer-only, and traditional hybrid federated models. The results underscore the efficacy of the proposed architecture as a scalable, privacy-conscious clinical decision-support system for collaborative multi-institutional disease risk assessment.

title: Quantum Gantt Charts for Spatial Data Engineering: Visualizing Hyperslab Bottlenecks, Pipeline Multiplication, and Quantum Candidate Stages
authors: Haque Munshi Mahidul
publish: 2026 Fourth International Conference on Distributed Computing and High Performance Computing (DCHPC)
abstract: Spatial data engineering pipelines routinely access multi-dimensional Earth-observation arrays by slicing regions-of-interest, time windows, and band subsets. In widely used array APIs, such subset access is expressed as a hyperslab selection. While hyperslabs are essential for partial access, they can also induce hyperslabbing overhead: repeated small subset reads that trigger disproportionate I/O, decompression, and orchestration costs relative to the numerical operations on the retrieved pixels. This paper develops a standards-grounded, visualization-first narrative based on Quantum Gantt Charts (QGantt) and Quantum Network Diagrams (QND). The manuscript contributes a compact diagram grammar tailored to spatial indexing, chunked storage, masks, and joins; three synthetic case studies with explicit numbers showing how hyperslabbing arises; a contrast between QGantt and BPMN/UML; a roadmap for a JavaScript prototype that renders QGantt from JSON into interactive diagrams; and a clear novelty statement contrasting QGantt with existing GIS workflow representations and quantum-inspired remote sensing work. A scope disclaimer prevents over-claims and keeps quantum references as annotations only.

title: From Pose to Verifiable Constraints: Robust Video Assessment of Early Post-THA Sit-to-Stand
authors: Sinan Chen, Shaojuan Huang, Xinyun Bai, Qiting Lin, Shuping Yu
publish: 2026 International Conference on Generative Artificial Intelligence and Information Security (GAIIS)
abstract: Early-stage rehabilitation after total hip arthroplasty (THA) increasingly relies on home/ward videos, yet pose-estimation noise and silent perturbations can yield over-confident motion-quality assessments. We propose a security-aware, constraint-optimized pipeline that converts a single RGB sit-to-stand (STS) clip into verifiable safety and interpretable quality outputs. A 2D pose estimator produces 17 keypoints per frame; reliability-aware gating and normalization enable robust temporal feature extraction (e.g., trunk angle, hip/knee angles, asymmetry, pelvis sway) and STS phase localization via vertical-velocity cues. Clinically grounded rules are formalized as hard constraints to generate a binary safety-validity indicator, while soft constraints are aggregated into a continuous quality score with penalty-based explanations. To address adversarial (or nuisance) keypoint-space perturbations, we evaluate robustness under controlled Gaussian noise and quantify score inflation and degradation; results reveal non-monotonic behavior that can inflate naive soft-constraint scores. We introduce reliability-aware regularization that couples penalty aggregation with a clip-level confidence ratio, mitigating inflation and stabilizing degradation trends. A pilot study on two post-operative day-3 THA patients recorded at Foshan Hospital of Traditional Chinese Medicine demonstrates clinically meaningful differentiation in asymmetry, trunk variability, and post-stand stability, while improving robustness against keypoint perturbations.

title: An Integrated Machine Learning Model for Accurate Stroke Diagnosis using CT Neuro Images
authors: B.Rama Subbaiah, Chenna Rajya Lakshmi, Kanike Lekha, Kummari Lavanya Kumari, Malipeddi Sai Tharun
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Electronic Devices and Intelligent Systems (ICSEDIS)
abstract: Stroke is one of the leading causes of death and disability in the world today and there is need to have quick and effective diagnostic methods. Computed Tomography (CT) is the preferred imaging modality in the assessment of acute stroke due to its availability and rapidity as well as the ability to distinguish ischemic and hemorrhagic lesions. Nevertheless, the manual interpretation process is time-intensive and prone to the variability, especially in the early subtle ischemic changes. This paper hypothesizes a multimodal machine learning platform that could incorporate neuroimaging, clinical and biochemical data in order to better detect and predict stroke. The process of extracting CT features is automated by the hybrid image encoder based on the EfficientNet and Vision Transformer (ViT) model, which allows capturing the details of the radiograph locally and the context on a large scale. Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) and TabTransformer models are used to model structured electronic health record (EHR) data in order to detect complex clinical relationships. Also bio-indicators of stroke risk and severity are provided with ferroptosis-and inflammation-related biomarkers as additional biological data. To produce a unified diagnostic output cross-modal attention based fusion layer carries out the integration of all learnt representations. It is found that experimental outcomes include increased accuracy, less dependence on the manual, and better stroke prediction based on personalization as opposed to the single-modality baselines. This method will facilitate quicker and more accurate stroke evaluation using simultaneous and clinically significant multimodal data, which can have significant advantages in emergency care and early intervention.

title: A 50–110 GHz UltraWideband Waveguide Diplexer Based on Novel Branch-Guide Coupler Utilizing Waveguide Cutoff Characteristics
authors: Yuchi Zhang, Bingli Dai, Yinian Feng, Zhongqian Niu, Yonghong Zhang, Bo Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques
abstract: This article presents a low-loss, high-isolation diplexer based on a novel ultrawideband branch-guide coupler (BGC). To maximize the operating bandwidth, a low-frequency transmission zero (TZ) is introduced by exploiting the waveguide cutoff characteristic, without incurring additional spurious responses. Furthermore, a dual-ridge waveguide branch structure is proposed, which generates a high-frequency TZ while reducing manufacturing complexity. For verification, a 12-branch BGC and a waveguide diplexer operating from 50 to 110 GHz were designed and fabricated. Measured results are in good agreement with simulations. For the BGC, the measured amplitude imbalance is below 0.9 dB (0.4 dB on average), and the return loss and isolation are better than 16 dB. For the diplexer, the measured insertion loss is below 1.3 dB for the 50–75 GHz band and below 0.9 dB for the 75–110 GHz band, with an isolation of 24 dB achieved between the two contiguous channels.

title: A Reference-Free Framework for Stereoscopic Image Quality Evaluation Using Wavelet and Sharpness Features of Scene Components_supp1-3693751.pdf
authors: Pankaj Kumar Raghuwanshi
publish: N/A
abstract: Stereoscopic-3D (S3D) technology has gained widespread adoption recently across multimedia-based consumer applications due to its ability to develop immersive perceptual experiences. With this tremendous utilization, assessing the generated S3D content quality is essential for achieving consistent and reliable performance across diverse multimedia systems and devices. To automate this process, we develop a reference-free image quality assessment (IQA) algorithm based on performing a multiscale biorthogonal wavelet transform on the cohesive color map derived from an S3D image. We model the resulting sub-bands using a univariate generalized Gaussian distribution and estimate the corresponding fitting coefficients. We demonstrate that the computed features effectively distinguish distortions and estimate the Chi-square distance between test image features and pristine model parameters to measure the primitive chrominance feature of an S3D image. Further, we perform a second derivative Laplacian operator on the cohesive color map to measure the overall sharpness of chromatic information of an S3D scene. Next, we compute the perceptual image quality evaluator on the left and right scenes to estimate the overall luminance feature of an S3D image. Finally, chromatic and luminance-based features are linearly combined to calculate the overall quality score of an S3D image. We evaluate our model on four benchmark datasets (LIVE Phase I & II, Waterloo Phase I & II), demonstrating its superior performance against eighteen existing IQA models, including 2D and 3D opinion-unaware and aware algorithms. This algorithm provides a highly effective solution to ensure the data quality required for the next generation of multimedia applications. The source code for the proposed opinion-unaware model can be accessed via the following: Google Drive link.

title: Tony Hoare: his path to the ACM Turing Award
authors: Krzysztof Apt
publish: Formal Aspects of Computing
abstract: None

title: ANTEATER: A Filter-then-Scrutinize Architecture for End-to-End Attack Investigation
authors: Yiming Ren, Haoqiang Wang, Linghao Li, Haoyang Chen, Chengxiang Si, Zhou Zhou, Qingyun Liu
publish: Proceedings of the ACM on Management of Data
abstract: None

title: LineRides: Line-Guided Reinforcement Learning for Bicycle Robot Stunts_supp1-3693936.mp4
authors: Seungeun Rho
publish: N/A
abstract: Designing reward functions for agile robotic maneuvers in reinforcement learning remains difficult, and demonstration-based approaches often require reference motions that are unavailable for novel platforms or extreme stunts. We present lineride, a line-guided learning framework that enables a custom bicycle robot to acquire diverse, commandable stunt behaviors from a user-provided spatial guideline and sparse key-orientations, without demonstrations or explicit timing. lineride handles physically infeasible guidelines using a tracking margin that permits controlled deviation, resolves temporal ambiguity by measuring progress via traveled distance along the guideline, and disambiguates motion details through position- and sequence-based key-orientations. We evaluate lineride on the Ultra Mobility Vehicle UMV and show that the policy trained with our methods supports seamless transitions between normal driving and stunt execution, enabling five distinct stunts on command: MiniHop, LargeHop, ThreePointTurn, Backflip, and DriftTurn.

title: A Robust Hybrid Deep Learning Approach for Lung Tumor Classification using Taguchi Optimization and Fuzzy Inference
authors: Indrish Pranav C K, Ajanthaa Lakkshmanan, Sri Nidhi A, Jesline. D, Ram Saravanan Kumaran
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Electronic Devices and Intelligent Systems (ICSEDIS)
abstract: Accurate early lung-tumor detection from computed tomography (CT) scans remains difficult due to noise, intensity variations, and borderline cases where tumor boundaries are visually ambiguous. This paper presents a hybrid deep learning framework named Adversarially Robust Taguchi-Optimized Fuzzy Convolutional Neural Network (ATF-CNN) for automated lung tumor classification. The pipeline begins with CT preprocessing for denoising and region enhancement, followed by CNN-based hierarchical feature extraction. To reduce trial-and-error tuning and improve training stability, Taguchi design of experiments is used to select robust hyperparameters efficiently. A fuzzy inference layer is then introduced to model uncertainty in deep features and to soften decision boundaries for difficult cases. In addition, adversarial training is incorporated to strengthen robustness against small input perturbations. The proposed model performs binary classification (tumor vs. non-tumor) and demonstrates strong performance in terms of accuracy, sensitivity, and AUC, indicating its suitability for computer-aided diagnostic support.

title: PACE - Plantard Accelerated CRYSTALS Extensions
authors: R. Bevin, Seogchung Seo, A. Khalid, M. O’Neill
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: ML-KEM (FIPS203) and ML-DSA (FIPS204) were two of the first post quantum cryptography schemes to be standardized by the National Institute of Standards and Technology; Both CRYSTALS algorithms utilize modulo reduction, specifically Montgomery reduction. Montgomery reduction traditionally is suited to computing moduli much larger than 32/64-bits and as such, the introduction of a new modulo reduction method published by Thomas Plantard allows for more efficient modulo reduction than Montgomery and Barrett as this new method allows for one less multiplication at the reduction stage. This is achieved by doubling the word size of the values being calculated, meaning the word size of data computed within CRYSTALS-Dilithium doubles from 32-bit to 64-bit and from 16-bit to 32-bit for CRYSTALS-Kyber. In this paper we present the worlds first Plantard ISEs ever that can compute both CRYSTALS-Kyber and CRYSTALS-Dilithium. Utilizing Plantard reduction as a set of Instruction Set Extensions, we are able to reduce the cycle counts required to compute the entire CRYSTALS-Kyber algorithm by up to 19.5% and reduce the cycle counts required to compute CRYSTALS-Dilithium by 26.6% as well as the CRYSTALS-Kyber INTT by 2.66× and the CRYSTALS-Dilithium INTT by 2.42×.

title: Data-Driven Probabilistic Evaluation of Admissible Heat-Source Fluctuation Ranges in District Heating Networks Using Measured Grid-Frequency Data
authors: Zhou Chengjian, Wang Zhonghao, Qin Huan, Gao Sijie
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: This study develops a data-driven probabilistic framework for evaluating admissible heat-source fluctuation ranges in district-heating networks. Measured grid-frequency-deviation data are first audited, conservatively cleaned, and standardized day by day to build a reliable empirical base process. A standardized disturbance library is then generated by moving block bootstrap so that the main non-Gaussian and temporal-correlation features are retained. The source-side heat-flow disturbance is represented in a unified (μ, σ<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>) parameter space, and admissibility is evaluated by Monte Carlo simulation using empirical feasibility probability instead of single-path judgment. Temperature-scan results from −20 °C to 0 °C show that both admissible envelopes expand as the weather becomes milder, but the data-driven envelope contracts earlier on the high-μ side, yielding a narrower feasible mean span and a lower variance tolerance in the upper operating range. The framework therefore supports a consistent comparison between ideal Gaussian disturbances and data-driven disturbances and provides a more realistic basis for assessing the stochastic operating capability of district-heating systems.

title: Boundary Constraint and Dynamic Graph Feature Decoupling for Weakly Supervised Semantic Segmentation
authors: Zhoufeng Liu, Bingrui Li, Chunlei Li, Shumin Ding, Miao Yu, Di Huang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Emerging Topics in Computational Intelligence
abstract: The weakly supervised semantic segmentation tasks have progressed through pseudo-labels derived from class activation maps. Most methods perform poorly in complex scenes, capturing local foreground features and relying on simple context. To address these challenges, we propose the boundary constraint and dynamic graph feature decoupling(BC-GFD) approach, which is designed to enhance foreground features representations through three key components: the Moment-Constrained Fusion strategy (MCFS), the Dynamic Graph Relational Mapping(DGRM) strategy, and the semantic difference measurement(SDM) strategy. Specifically, MCFS is designed to emphasize and constrain non-discriminative regions, while DGRM manages long-range dependencies between global contextual semantics to extract global fine-grained features. Meanwhile, SDM focuses on quantifying foreground-background disparity, enhancing the ability to decouple local fine-grained features. BC-GFD effectively focuses on constraining boundary features and integrating global-local complementary foreground features to enhance adaptability to complex contexts. Extensive experiments on the PASCAL VOC 2012 and MS COCO 2014 demonstrate the effectiveness of our method and achieve the state-of-the-art performance.

title: Hierarchical Event-Triggered Cooperative Control of Heterogeneous Vehicle Platoons Under Switching Topologies
authors: Zhiping Shen, Huarui Guo, Yilin Wu, Weikang Yang, Yonggui Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: This paper studies the cooperative control problem for heterogeneous connected vehicle platoons (HCVPs) under switching topologies, which do not require each subgraph to be necessarily connected. First, to effectively save communication costs, a hierarchical control framework composed of an upper-level event-triggered observer (ETO) layer and a lower-level local tracking controller layer is proposed. Within the proposed framework, an ETO for each follower vehicle is designed to observe the leader, and a local tracking controller is designed using the proposed observer to ensure that each follower has the tracking capability of their own observer. Second, to improve the robustness of the system, a completely distributed control protocol is designed for jointly connected undirected topologies, which does not require global network information and is independent of switching rules. Meanwhile, to overcome the challenge of constructing Lyapunov function, a new convergence analysis method based on the contraction argument is employed for jointly connected directed topologies to solve the cooperative control problem of the vehicle platoon (VP). Third, the absence of Zeno-behavior in any finite time can be verified by showing that the event interval between any two consecutive triggering instants is strictly greater than zero. Finally, simulations are used to verify the effectiveness of the proposed protocols.

title: FPGA-Based Real-Time Embedded Vision System with Gigabit Ethernet Communication
authors: Yang Zhou
publish: 2026 International Conference on Generative Artificial Intelligence and Information Security (GAIIS)
abstract: Real-time image processing and low-latency data transmission are essential requirements for embedded vision systems in edge computing scenarios. This paper presents an FPGA-based real-time embedded vision system implemented on the Anlogic EG4S20 platform. The proposed system builds a full-hardware accelerated pipeline covering image acquisition, real-time image processing, and Gigabit Ethernet data transmission. Multiple visual tasks are realized in parallel hardware architecture, including Sobel edge detection, color-based object detection, shape classification, and motion trajectory tracking with fade-out effects. To support stable video streaming, an asynchronous FIFO cross-clock domain buffering scheme and a lightweight UDP packetization mechanism are designed for real-time Gigabit Ethernet transmission. Furthermore, the system supports direct peer-to-peer Ethernet communication between two FPGA boards, providing a feasible prototype for distributed embedded vision applications. Experimental results show that the system achieves a stable frame rate of 12-13 fps at 640x480 resolution, with an end-to-end latency below 100 ms and optimized resource utilization. While the observed frame rate is limited by host-side rendering overhead, the internal FPGA hardware pipeline achieves a deterministic processing latency of less than 3 ms, demonstrating high intrinsic throughput. The proposed design validates the efficiency of FPGA-based parallel acceleration for real-time embedded vision and shows good potential for industrial edge vision applications.

title: IEEE Standard for Energy Efficient Dynamic Line Rate Transmission System
authors: N/A
publish: N/A
abstract: An energy-efficient, rate and modulation adaptive transmission system designed for the deployment of mixed line rates in optical networks is specified by this standard. The transmission system is introduced by this standard, and mechanisms to optimally configure the line rate to efficiently accommodate varying traffic loads while reducing the power consumption are provided.

title: Asymptotic Boundedness of Distributed Set-Membership Filtering
authors: Yudong Li, Yirui Cong, Shimin Wang, Martin Guay, Jiuxiang Dong
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automatic Control
abstract: Asymptotic boundedness is a crucial property of Distributed Set-Membership Filtering (DSMFing) that prevents the unbounded growth of the set estimates caused by the wrapping effect. However, this important property remains underinvestigated, compared to its noise-free and stochastic-noise counterparts, i.e., the convergence of Distributed Observers (DOs) and the bounded error covariance of Distributed Kalman Filters (DKFs). This paper studies the asymptotic boundedness of DSMFing for linear discrete-time systems. A novel concept, termed the Distributed Observation-Information Tower (DOIT), is introduced to characterize the fundamental relationship between the structure of graphs and the set estimates, which enables the boundedness analysis. Leveraging the DOIT, an easily verifiable sufficient condition for the asymptotic boundedness of linear DSMFing is established. Surprisingly, the sufficient condition generalizes the well-known collective detectability condition for DOs and DKFs; it links DSMFs to existing distributed estimation methods and reveals the unique characteristic of DSMFs.

title: Role-based Adaptive Coordination for Resilient Signal Coverage in Stratospheric Balloons
authors: Zexin Wang, Zhenglin Liang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: Superpressure balloons in the stratosphere are emerging as a promising platform for extending network connectivity to remote and disaster-affected regions. Yet, their dependence on altitude control and absence of horizontal maneuverability render them highly susceptible to wind drift, leading to unstable coverage. Coordinating multiple balloons further challenges current multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) methods, as wind-induced moderation effects can simultaneously disrupt all agents. Here we present a novel Adaptive Role-Switching Cooperative Learning (ARSCL), a dual-policy MARL framework that achieves resilient, cooperative coverage through adaptive role specialization. ARSCL assigns complementary roles —a main policy maintaining primary coverage and a compensatory policy introducing diversity to counteract correlated drift—linked by a dynamic role-switching mechanism that adapts to spatial deviation. A drift-prevention module further constrains learning to feasible trajectories. Simulations demonstrate that ARSCL achieves an average coverage duration exceeding 94% within a 25 km radius over a 24-hour period, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art MARL baselines in both efficiency and resilience.

title: Multi Person Localization and Breathing Rate Estimation using Translational and Rotational Synthetic Aperture Radar
authors: Ashutosh Deshwal, Alireza Azizi, Shubham Sinha, Divyanshu Pandey, Nishant Mehrotra, Amitangshu Pal, Ashutosh Sabharwal
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Imaging
abstract: Reliable detection and localization of humans through structural obstructions is critical for time-sensitive applications such as disaster response. This paper presents a novel ultra-wideband (UWB) radar-based framework for multi-person localization and breathing rate estimation in obstructed environments. To overcome the challenges posed by signal attenuation, multipath interference, and limited spatial resolution, we propose a synthetic aperture radar (SAR) system that combines linear translation and on-axis rotation of a single radar. By fusing intensity maps from multiple radar viewpoints, the system achieves enhanced localization of human targets. A signal quality assessment pipeline, including SNR-based filtering, dominance ratio analysis, and periodicity checks, is used to extract clean respiratory signals from selected range bins. The estimates are further integrated into a spatial breathing-rate matrix, which is fused with radar motion maps to suppress false detections and improve reliability. The proposed algorithm is validated experimentally under line-of-sight, through-wall, and emulated rubble conditions. Results demonstrate significant improvements in localization accuracy and robustness of respiration-based human presence detection. The proposed post-processing filtering algorithms reduce the 80<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">th</sup> percentile Chamfer distance by 1.53×, while the maximum Chamfer distance is reduced by <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\sim 3\times$</tex-math></inline-formula>. The estimated breathing rate correlates strongly with ground truth while achieving a Pearson correlation of 0.79.

title: High Selectivity Superconducting Dual-Band Bandpass Filter Using Degenerate Modes of Capacitively Loaded Double Ring Resonators
authors: Xinlei Liu, Zhewang Ma, Baoping Ren, Bingqing Miao
publish: IEEE Microwave and Wireless Technology Letters
abstract: In this letter, a capacitively loaded double ring resonator (CL-DRR) with a pair of degenerate modes is proposed. Compared with conventional dual-mode ring resonators, its size is reduced by 86.5%, and its two modes can be controlled independently even though they are closely neighbored. When the CL-DRRs are coupled in different orientations, distinctive coupling characteristics are obtained, and multiple transmission zeros (TZs) are produced. By using dual-tapped feedlines with a compensation tuning technique, the independent control of the external quality factors of the dual passbands is achieved. A compact dual-band bandpass filter (BPF) with a small center-frequency ratio is designed, realizing independent control of both its center frequencies and bandwidths, together with sharp roll-off passbands due to its multiple TZs. The measured response of the fabricated filter using high-temperature superconducting (HTS) films satisfies the design specifications well and shows very low loss.

title: Blockchain based Electronic Health Record (EHR) Management
authors: Shankar Gugulothu, D. Sandhya Rani, L. Swathi, K. Mrudul, Y. Kavya, K. Uday Kumar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Electronic Devices and Intelligent Systems (ICSEDIS)
abstract: The growing use of digital healthcare has raised the demand for a secured and efficient way of handling the data in the health records. The Electronic Health Record (EHR) Management System based on the blockchain technology represents an innovative approach that has been developed for addressing the issues like possibility of ruptures, lack of transparency, and the absence of patients’ control in the patients’ hands currently present in the prevailing EHR Management Systems. For the proposed project, an EHR Management System will be developed that will be decentralized and capable enough to ensure the secured storage of health records of the patients, along with their efficient sharing within their authorized health care organizations through the use of the blockchain technology. The use of blockchain technology will ensure the EHRs possess the strongest characteristics like immutability, increased level of transparency, and the highest level of smart contract-based management regarding the right of health records’ sharing. The storage of the health files will be done through the storage utilization of the blockchain-based hashes.

title: Approximating local properties by tensor network states with constant bond dimension
authors: Yichen Huang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Information Theory
abstract: Classical simulation of quantum many-body systems is a fundamental challenge due to their exponentially large Hilbert spaces. Tensor network states are a powerful ansatz to efficiently represent many physically relevant quantum states. A key question is the bond dimension—which determines the number of parameters in the ansatz—required to approximate all local properties to accuracy δ. In one dimension, we prove that an area law for the Rényi entanglement entropy Rα with index α < 1 implies a matrix product state representation with bond dimension poly(1/δ). For (at most constant-fold degenerate) ground states of one-dimensional gapped Hamiltonians, a bond dimension almost linear in 1/δ suffices. In two dimensions, an area law for Rα(α < 1) implies a projected entangled pair state representation with bond dimension eO(1/δ). In both one and two dimensions, analogous results are obtained for states with logarithmic corrections to the area law. These findings rigorously justify the common practice of using a system-size-independent bond dimension in tensor network simulations.

title: Research on Genetic Algorithm Optimization for Job-shop Scheduling Problem
authors: Jie Lv, Zewen Lai, Yuanjia Zhang
publish: 2026 International Conference on Generative Artificial Intelligence and Information Security (GAIIS)
abstract: The research on job-shop scheduling problems is crucial in the field of manufacturing production optimization. The focus of such research is to achieve specific production goals by reasonably arranging task processing sequences and machine allocation. Based on traditional genetic algorithm, this paper optimizes the traditional algorithm through recursive neural network simulation of fitness function and simulated annealing hybrid algorithm. Through benchmark studies, it is found that the hybrid algorithm yields better results.

title: Synthetic Aperture Radar Image Change Detection Based on Global Dynamic Context-Aware Network
authors: Baogui Huan, Chuanzheng Gong, Dezhong Chen, Feng Gao, Junyu Dong, Qian Du
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Convolutional neural networks (CNNs) have been extensively and successfully applied to the task of synthetic aperture radar (SAR) image change detection. However, conventional convolutional layers are inherently limited by their local receptive fields, which mainly capture spatially localized patterns while neglecting the global context that is often crucial for accurately distinguishing subtle or large-scale changes in SAR imagery. Moreover, developing robust and stable detection models in this domain remains particularly challenging due to the scarcity of annotated training samples, which frequently lead to overfitting and unstable convergence during training. To address these limitations, we propose a novel Global Dynamic Context-Aware Network (GDNet) specifically tailored for SAR image change detection. At the core of our approach lies a novel global dynamic convolution module, which adaptively modulates convolution kernel weights according to the global semantic information extracted from the input features. By dynamically incorporating long-range dependencies, this mechanism enables the network to integrate both local detail and global context, thus improving its ability to detect diverse change patterns. In addition, we intro duce a carefully designed two-stage Mixup strategy for model training. Unlike conventional single-stage Mixup, our two-stage design generates more diverse and informative training samples, effectively regularizing the model and yielding more stable and reliable classification results even under limited data scenarios. Extensive experiments on three SAR datasets demonstrate the superiority of the proposed GDNet compared to other state-of the-art methods. These findings highlight the potential of global dynamic modeling and advanced data augmentation strategies for advancing SAR image interpretation. Source codes are available at https://github.com/oucailab/GDNet.

title: SAR Image Change Detection Based on Saliency Guidance and Edge Enhancement
authors: Shuanghui Wang, Zhihui Xin, Jianxiang Zeng, Runze Song
publish: 2026 International Conference on Generative Artificial Intelligence and Information Security (GAIIS)
abstract: SAR image change detection is a key remote sensing technology, but it suffers from weak change target indistinctness, edge blurriness and poor robustness in complex noise environments. To address these issues, this paper proposes a SAR image change detection method based on saliency guidance and structure enhancement, following the "localization first, refinement later" idea. Firstly, a high-quality difference map is generated via multi-scale difference construction and structural response enhancement to strengthen the response of weak change targets. Secondly, a saliency detection mechanism is introduced to adaptively screen candidate change regions, globally suppressing background noise and non-structural interference. Finally, gradient and edge structure information are integrated to compensate details and enhance contours of salient regions, restoring the boundary information of change regions. Experimental results show that the method effectively resolves edge blurriness and target breakage in traditional methods, improves the separability between changed and unchanged regions, and enhances robustness in complex scenarios, providing an efficient solution for SAR image change detection.

title: Viscoelastic modeling of the magnetic losses in punch-degraded non-oriented electrical steel
authors: Benjamin Ducharne, Floran Martin, Anouar Belahcen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Magnetics
abstract: This study examines the effect of punching on magnetic losses in non-oriented electrical steel using a viscoelastic model based on fractional-derivative operators. Specimens with identical material volume and increasing edge-affected area were characterized under alternating magnetization with controlled sinusoidal flux density. The frequency dependence of losses is described by a fractional order n and a coefficient ρ, related to viscoelastic mechanisms and material properties, respectively. The results show an almost monotonic increase of n with edge-affected area, providing a compact indicator of cutting-induced degradation, while ρ remains close to analytically predicted values. A scaling analysis links the experimental configurations to 1 kW transformers and motors. Loss predictions at elevated frequencies highlight the significant contribution of punching-induced degradation under practical operating conditions.

title: Unified Metric Learning for Personalized Fashion Recommendations with Fast User Adaptation
authors: Zhi Lu, Yang Hu, Cong Yu, Yan Chen, Bing Zeng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia
abstract: Personlized fashion recommendation aims to model outfit composition quality and recommend compatible item combinations for users. However, existing studies often address outfit-level and item-level recommendations as separate problems, resulting in fragmented formulations. In this work, we revisit these problems from a unified mathematical perspective and extend fashion recommendation into a coherent family of composition tasks. These tasks, including outfit recommendation, outfit completion, outlier item detection, and new user profiling, are formulated within a common probabilistic framework that differs only in search constraints or conditioning. Based on this unified view, we propose a metric learning framework that embeds items, outfits, and users into a shared space where outfit compatibility is modeled as the probability of co-occurrence under composition utility. The learned embeddings provide consistent evaluation across tasks, enable personalized recommendation, and support fast user adaptation through plug-and-play user parameters without fine-tuning. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple composition tasks and establishes a unified foundation for generalizable and adaptive fashion recommendation.

title: AI-Powered Skill Assessment: Personalized Feedback Through Video and Emotion Analysis
authors: A.Priyanka, A.Chiranjeevi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Electronic Devices and Intelligent Systems (ICSEDIS)
abstract: The traditional skill assessment techniques that involve written tests and manual skill assessment analysis are not effective enough to capture the real-time learning behavior and emotional engagement of the learners, leading to incomplete analysis of skill performance. The primary aim of this research work is to develop an intelligent and adaptive skill assessment system that not only analyzes the proficiency of knowledge but also captures the emotional and behavioral aspects of the learners during the skill assessment process. This research work aims to develop an AI-powered skill assessment system that combines dynamic question generation, video emotion analysis and personalized feedback delivery through an interactive user interface. The proposed system uses a language model-driven approach to develop adaptive assessment questions, and a deep learning-based convolutional neural network approach to analyze live video feeds and identify facial emotions such as confidence, stress, focus and hesitation. The user responses, emotional conditions, and performance measures are constantly tracked and recorded for secure storage in a structured database. The result of the experimental assessment proves that the emotion recognition model has an accuracy of 92.4%, whereas the skill evaluation and performance classification module has an accuracy of 94.1%.

title: Physics-based self-supervised learning of a deep network for single-shot in-line hologram reconstruction
authors: Dylan Brault, Félix Riedel, Corinne Fournier, Thomas Olivier, Loïc Denis
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Imaging
abstract: Digital in-line holographic microscopy is a computational imaging method useful for characterizing the refractive properties of a sample, i.e. the phase shift and absorption. This indirect measurement technique captures a diffraction pattern and uses reconstruction algorithms to retrieve the optical properties of the sample. Since only the intensity of the diffracted wave is recorded on the sensor, this inversion is not trivial, and simple backward propagation leads to artifacts known in optics as the “twin-image”. With advances in deep learning, various algorithms have been developed for the reconstruction of in-line holograms, providing computationally efficient alternatives to iterative algorithms. These algorithms rely either on supervised learning, which requires ground truth knowledge, or physics-based self-supervised algorithms that require additional information, like phase diversity, but require multiple holograms for inference. This paper introduces a new self-supervised physics-based deep learning strategy that leverages phase diversity during training and then reconstructs sample's transmission function from a single in-line hologram during inference. We introduce five datasets of simulated and experimental in-line holograms of beads and bacteria. The proposed method produces accurate quantitative reconstructions similar or even more accurate than those obtained by regularized inversion while reducing the computational time by a factor of 1000.

title: Evolving Diverse Red-team Language Models in Multi-round Multi-agent Games
authors: Chengdong Ma, Ziran Yang, Hai Ci, Jun Gao, Minquan Gao, Xuehai Pan, Yaodong Yang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: The primary challenge in deploying Large Language Model (LLM) is ensuring its harmlessness. Red team can identify vulnerabilities by attacking LLM to attain safety. However, current efforts heavily rely on single-round prompt designs and unilateral red team optimizations against fixed blue teams. These static approaches lead to significant reductions in generation diversity, known as the mode collapse, which makes it difficult to discover the potential risks in the increasingly complex human-LLM interactions. Here we introduce dynamic Red Team Game (RTG) to comprehensively analyze the multi-round offensive and defensive interactions between red team and blue team. Furthermore, we develop a Gamified Red Team Solver (GRTS) with diversity measures to mitigate mode collapse and theoretically guarantee the convergence of approximate Nash equilibrium which results in better strategies for both teams. Empirical results demonstrate that GRTS explore diverse and implicit attacks to adaptively exploit various LLMs, surpassing the constraints of specific modes. Insightfully, the geometrical structure we unveil of the red team task aligns with the spinning top hypothesis, confirming the necessity of constructing a diverse LLM population as a promising proxy for heterogeneous human expert red-teamers. This paves the way for scalable toxicity detection and safe alignment for LLMs.

title: Shielded Med-AI: Privacy-Centric Federated Deep Learning for Healthcare Imaging
authors: Thripthi P Balakrishnan, Gangaraju Vamil, Bommadi Vanitha, Nara Vasantha, Somarpu Sowmya Sree
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Electronic Devices and Intelligent Systems (ICSEDIS)
abstract: Federated learning is now an attractive method of privacy protection in the analysis of medical images. It allows collaborative model training without the requirement to reveal personal patient data. Nevertheless, the balance between diagnostics quality and patient data confidentiality is still a considerable issue, and especially in large healthcare facilities. This paper describes Shielded Med-AI, a privacy-conscious federated deep learning model of multi-disease medical image classification in multiple healthcare organizations. The system uses convolutional neural networks (CNNs) to perform local training on the patient data. The encrypted model updates are sent to the central aggregation server which uses the Federated Averaging algorithm. Noise injection and gradient clipping are applied in order to enhance privacy with the help of differential privacy. There are reduced risks of information leakage through this strategy. Besides, the framework employs weighted aggregation and data augmentation to manage non-IID data distributions. In addition, explainability through Grad-Cam is added to improve clinical transparency and trust by providing the visual understanding of model predictions. This method according to the experimental results preserves a small accuracy loss to enhance privacy with an accuracy of 93.3 in centrally managed environments, 92.1 in federated learning, and 90.8 with different privacy. This is a safe and easy-to-use method and suitable in real healthcare facilities.

title: Performance Evaluation of Approximate Multipliers for Energy-Efficient Computing Systems
authors: Bhuvana B P, Pown M, V Monishkumar, P Sairam, Bharathi Raju
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Electronic Devices and Intelligent Systems (ICSEDIS)
abstract: The paper introduces the deployment and performance analysis of approximate multiplier architectures and compares them to the traditional Array, Wallace and Vedic multipliers 4x4 and 8x8 on FPGA. The comparison will be done regarding hardware utilization and propagation delay. It has been shown through experimental results that the approximate multiplier incurs a smaller number of slices and LUTs than the architectures in both designs. In addition, the proposed design has a lower critical path delay, making it compute time-saving. The results of the observed improvements confirm that the controlled approximation can be effectively used to decrease the complexity of hardware, but the computational performance is not worse. The paper confirms that approximate multipliers are quite appropriate in a situation where small calculation errors can be afforded with great value in return of huge gains in area efficiency and speed, and is thus a good contender to both low-power and high-performance VLSI systems.
