title: GATEWAY: A Game Theory Model for Analyzing NYC Road Tolls and Commuter Response
authors: Hailun Wu, Ziqian Dong, Roberto Rojas-Cessa
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: Road toll policies, such as the newly implemented congestion pricing in New York City, are effective measures in both reducing traffic congestion and generating revenue. However, pursuing these two objectives requires understanding how toll policy changes influence commuters’ travel mode choices. Existing tools aim to estimate car travel demand but overlook the trade-off between traffic reduction and revenue generation. To address this gap, we propose GATEWAY, a Stackelberg game-based model where a policymaker introduces a toll, and commuters respond to the toll by selecting a travel mode. By identifying the Nash equilibrium, where the objectives of the policymaker and the commuters travel mode choice are optimized, GATEWAY suggests a toll and estimates the respective travel demand. The accuracy of GATEWAY was validated through two case studies: the New York City’s bridge and tunnel toll increase in 2010 and the congestion pricing program implemented in 2025. Our results show high consistency in the estimation of car travel demand and consumer surplus compared to the actual traffic volume reported by the Metropolitan Transportation Authority and a transportation-policy multi-agent virtual testbed, respectively. GATEWAY can also predict the reduction in traffic and the revenue generation of future toll increases.

title: On The Co-polarized Hannan's Limit for Circularly Polarized Arrays
authors: Zitong Wang, Qi Wu
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: Embedded element efficiency (EEE), which characterizes the gain utilization of elements in large planar arrays, has a fundamental upper bound known as Hannan's limit. This limit was originally derived for linearly polarized arrays. In this Letter, we extend Hannan's limit to circularly polarized (CP) arrays, accounting for the amplitude and phase constraints required to generate CP waves. Inherent amplitude and phase errors in feed networks, along with mutual coupling among array elements, are analyzed. Both factors constrain the achievable EEE bound. A compensation method of the amplitude and phase errors is proposed to approach the extended Hannan's limit. Findings of this Letter can assist the optimization of CP arrays in a limited space, which is critical for satellite navigation and communication applications.

title: A Study on Information Security of Internal Communication Bus in Fire Automatic Alarm Systems
authors: Xiaobai Li, Yu Shao, Yingcong Zhang, Jiansong Wang, Zibo Yuan, Chen Zhong
publish: 2026 International Conference on Generative Artificial Intelligence and Information Security (GAIIS)
abstract: The control bus of fire equipment serves as a critical communication infrastructure for automatic fire alarm systems, whose security performance directly determines the reliability of early warning, cooperative control, and emergency disposal. However, conventional bus communication mechanisms rarely consider resource constraints, real-time limitations, and heterogeneous bus requirements, resulting in potential threats such as identity spoofing, data tampering, replay attacks, and unauthorized access. To address these issues, this paper proposes a lightweight and highly efficient security protection scheme for fire equipment control buses. This paper proposes a lightweight information security protection scheme, constructing an integrated security architecture for the fire equipment control bus that includes bidirectional identity authentication, selective Chinese national cryptographic encryption, and dual-level integrity verification. Referring to the technical content of GB/T 32907-2016 "Information Security Technology — SM4 Block Cipher Algorithm", the scheme adopts a lightweight optimized Chinese national cryptographic SM4 block cipher algorithm and a simplified SHA-256 hash algorithm. Under the premise of meeting the hard real-time constraints of the bus, it achieves trusted authentication of communication nodes, encrypted message transmission, and integrity verification. Experimental results demonstrate that the presented scheme can completely defend against identity spoofing, data tampering, replay attacks, and unauthorized access. The additional communication latency is limited to 3.6 ms for the CAN bus and 17.6 ms for the RS485 bus, which fully meets the real-time and resource-constrained requirements of fire equipment systems. This research provides a practical, lightweight, and deployable security solution for automatic fire alarm systems, fire linkage control systems, and intelligent fire protection projects.. (Abstract)

title: A Proactive Ransomware Defense Model for SSDs Based on AVEIO Features and Random Forest
authors: Hua Liang, Wei Li, Feng Yang, Shuang Gao
publish: 2026 International Conference on Generative Artificial Intelligence and Information Security (GAIIS)
abstract: Faced with the escalating threats of ransomware and the inherent limitations of traditional host-layer defenses in terms of privilege escalation and resource consumption, this paper proposes a ransomware defense model based on multi-dimensional I/O features at the Solid-State Drive (SSD) storage layer. Leveraging the hardware isolation advantages of SSDs at the bottom of the I/O path, the proposed model delves into internal I/O mechanisms and introduces novel features such as Average Erasure Size (AVEIO) and Accumulative Effect Coefficient (ACCEIO) alongside traditional entropy and erasure metrics. The model employs a Random Forest algorithm to construct a lightweight classifier and incorporates a real-time detection strategy based on continuous time windows to enhance robustness, aiming to bridge the "semantic gap" in storage-layer detection and effectively distinguish malicious encryption from legitimate activities of secure erasure tools like SDelete. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed model accurately characterizes the Read-after-Write and high-entropy data stream patterns of ransomware, significantly improving generalization for variants with minimal overhead, thereby providing a new perspective for building reliable underlying data security defense systems.

title: Accurate Blood Group Identification Through Fingerprint Analysis using Vision Transformer
authors: Sivasankar Rajamani P, Gowri Shankar C, Sridevi S, Abinaya T, Gowsick B, Hariprasanth M
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Electronic Devices and Intelligent Systems (ICSEDIS)
abstract: Fingerprint images for blood group designation do not provide much value because of insufficient knowledge of global ridge dependencies while existing approaches using CNNs mostly consider local features. This research article presents an approach based on a Vision Transformer (ViT), which allows for the capture of long-range spatial dependencies in fingerprint images. It was trained using 8000 images obtained from with 6400 images for training and 1600 images for testing. After evaluating the developed model against a CNN baseline (85.93%) with an average accuracy of 92.81%, statistical analysis results were found to be significant (p < 0.05). The proposed model demonstrates superior performance with 96.50% precision, 96.60% recall, and 96.20% F1-score. Furthermore, the proposed system will take 0.3–0.5 seconds to predict blood group via a single fingerprint image. Thus, there is evidence of improved stability and generalization ability for the ViT architecture as a non-invasive method for predicting blood groups. The Vision Transformer did better than the CNN model at predicting a person's blood type from their fingerprints without needing to take a blood sample.

title: A Hybrid GAE-DNN Framework with Slime Mould Optimization for Lung Cancer Prediction
authors: Srisoundaryaa S S P, Rajesh Kanna P, Dhivya P, Soundhiri S
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Electronic Devices and Intelligent Systems (ICSEDIS)
abstract: Lung cancer remains one of the leading causes of cancer-related mortality worldwide due to delayed diagnosis and the complex relationships among clinical risk factors. Accurate prediction using structured clinical data is challenging because conventional machine learning and standard deep learning models often treat features independently and rely on manually selected hyperparameters. This study aims to develop a reliable predictive framework that models relationships among clinical attributes and improves lung cancer risk prediction. Existing systems primarily employ traditional classifiers such as Logistic Regression, Support Vector Machines, Random Forests, and gradient-boosting models, which provide moderate performance but fail to capture interdependencies among clinical variables. This study utilizes a structured clinical dataset containing 5,000 patient records and 18 attributes, including demographic, lifestyle, symptom-based, and physiological factors. The proposed approach introduces a hybrid learning framework that integrates Graph Autoencoder (GAE) representation learning, Deep Neural Network (DNN) classification, and Slime Mould Optimization (SMO) for hyperparameter tuning. Initially, the numerical clinical data are transformed into a similarity-based graph structure to represent relationships among features. The GAE learns compact latent embeddings that preserve structural dependencies, which are then used by the DNN classifier for lung cancer prediction. SMO is applied to optimize network parameters and improve training stability. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed GAE–DNN–SMO model achieves an accuracy of 0.92, precision of 0.91, recall of 0.93, and F1-score of 0.92, outperforming conventional machine learning models. The framework provides reliable prediction outcomes and supports clinical decision-making by generating probability-based risk assessments. Future work will focus on integrating multimodal clinical data and improving model interpretability for practical healthcare applications.

title: Adaptive Resource Allocation in Distributed Systems Using Reinforcement Learning and Graph Neural Networks
authors: Alireza Rahimipour Anaraki, Parvaneh Asghari
publish: 2026 Fourth International Conference on Distributed Computing and High Performance Computing (DCHPC)
abstract: In modern distributed computing environments, efficient resource allocation remains a challenging problem due to dynamic workloads, heterogeneous resources, and complex inter-task dependencies. Traditional scheduling heuristics (e.g., First-Come-First-Served and Round Robin) often fail to adapt to such conditions or to exploit structural information in job execution graphs. This paper presents an integrated framework for adaptive resource allocation that combines reinforcement learning (RL) with graph neural networks (GNNs) to learn scheduling policies in distributed systems. The GNN component encodes graph-structured system state—such as task dependency graphs and resource availability—into compact representations, enabling the RL agent to generalize across varying workloads and system topologies. Based on these representations, the RL agent selects scheduling actions (e.g., task selection or placement) with the objective of optimizing long-term performance metrics, including throughput, job completion time, resource utilization, and fairness. We describe the design of the proposed RL+GNN framework, covering state and action formulation as well as reward design, and provide a simulation-based conceptual evaluation in a cloud/HPC cluster scheduling setting. The results suggest that incorporating graph-based state representations into reinforcement learning can improve the adaptivity and scalability of resource allocation strategies, particularly in environments with complex dependency structures and dynamic behavior.

title: Centralized Droop-Controlled PV Multi-Microgrid System Employing a Modified Ten-Switch Inverter with Reduced CMV, Leakage Current, and Losses
authors: Kotb B. Tawfiq, Hatem Zeineldin, Ahmed Al-Durra, Ehab F. El-Saadany
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industry Applications
abstract: This paper presents a Modified Ten-Switch Inverter (MTSI) applied in a photovoltaic (PV) multi-microgrid system to reduce common-mode voltage (CMV), leakage current, and power losses. A centralized droop-control framework is used to coordinate multiple distributed inverters and ensure stable operation, accurate active and reactive power sharing, and seamless interaction with the utility grid. The control strategy is intentionally kept conventional to highlight the impact of the proposed inverter topology at the system level. Simulation and experimental results confirm that the MTSI limits the CMV within  ± V<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">dc</sub>/6, suppresses leakage current to 47.4–61.1 mA (well below IEC standard limits), and maintains low current distortion with a THD below 1.2%. The total inverter losses range from 101.4 W to 109.5 W over a DC-link voltage span of 800–1400 V, representing a 15.8% reduction compared with existing ten-switch inverter. Stable voltage and frequency regulation and smooth operation under both islanded and grid-connected conditions are achieved without controller reconfiguration. These results demonstrate that the proposed MTSI provides a compact, efficient, and EMI-compliant solution for PV-based multi-microgrid systems.

title: Frequency-Attention-Multiscale Network (FAMNet): A Deep Learning Framework for Robust Multiclass Classification of Kidney CT Images
authors: R. Raja Subramanian, Sri Ram Ganesh M, Arockia Sathiya Dass J, Gayathri Devi.M, S.M.KomathiSathya, Matli Jai Hind
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Electronic Devices and Intelligent Systems (ICSEDIS)
abstract: The accuracy of classifying renal pathology abnormalities using CT imaging continues to be challenging because of noise, low contrast, and extremely limited structural variations between different pathological classes. This paper describes FAMNet (Frequency-Attention-Multiscale Network), a deep learning architecture operating on both dual domains, which uses frequency and spatial information to improve clinical diagnosis. The FAMNet framework utilizes a comprehensive preprocessing pipeline consisting of non-local means (NLM) for denoising, CLAHE-based contrast enhancement, unsharp masking and Haar wavelet decomposition to create rich four-channel (four-band) image representations. The FAMNet model consists of two types of branches, spatial and frequency, and they are integrated using a dual cross-attention mechanism. The dual cross-attention mechanism allows local anatomical textures and global spectral patterns to interact via contextual exchanges. The FAMNet model achieved 99% accuracy among normal, cysts, tumors, and stones across many images with extensive augmentation and class balancing. It outperformed existing deep learning architectures (EfficientNet, DenseNet, MobileNet, etc.) in terms of both accuracy and error metrics; therefore, its results demonstrate the FAMNet model's robustness and generalization capabilities. A generative AI component is integrated into this application, which converts model predictions into structured medical documentation for the automated clinic workflow process. Therefore, a complete pipeline exists for diagnosing kidney images. The study results suggest that the combination of attention-based refining and spatial-frequency fusing results in improved diagnostic precision.

title: A Finite-Time Distributed Control Protocol for Multiagent Placement to Spatiotemporal Points$^\star$
authors: Deniz Kurtoglu, Tansel Yucelen, Dzung Tran, David Casbeer, Eloy Garcia
publish: IEEE Transactions on Control of Network Systems
abstract: A broad class of multiagent system applications such as environmental monitoring, information gathering and resource extraction, transportation and delivery, and task assignment requires agents to separately visit specific locations in space at specific moments in time (i.e., spatiotemporal points). Motivated by this standpoint, the contribution of this paper is a finite-time distributed control protocol over directed graphs to address the problem of multiagent placement to spatiotemporal points. Using methods from time transformation, input-to-state stability, and Lyapunov stability, it has been shown that the proposed protocol is capable of driving the trajectories of agents to the given spatiotemporal points at different user-defined times. Two illustrative numerical examples are also provided to showcase the efficacy of the proposed protocol.

title: Low-Threshold and High-Bandwidth Green InGaN Hybrid QW-QD-like VCSELs for Visible-Light and Chip-to-Chip Optical Interconnects
authors: Tzu-Yi Lee, Chien-Chi Huang, Fu-He Hsiao, Zi-Ran You, Li-Yin Chen, Ching-Hsueh Chiu, Yu-Heng Hong, Hao-Chung Kuo
publish: IEEE Photonics Journal
abstract: The increasing demand for high-speed data transmission in artificial intelligence computing and data-center architectures has accelerated the development of compact optical interconnect technologies. In this work, a green InGaN hybrid quantum well-quantum-dot like vertical-cavity surface-emitting laser (hybrid QW-QD-like VCSEL) operating near 510 nm is demonstrated. The device incorporates an InGaN active region and a nanoporous GaN distributed Bragg reflector (NP-DBR) to form a vertical resonant cavity. Optical characterization indicates strong carrier localization behavior and hybrid QW-QD-like recombination in the active region. The fabricated device with a 12 μm aperture exhibits a low threshold current of 1.032 mA (0.9125 kA/cm²) and stable lasing near 510 nm. High-speed modulation measurements show a -3dB bandwidth of 3.18 GHz, and data transmission experiments achieve a bit-error rate of 2.6 × 10⁻³ at 4.5 Gb/s, below the forward-error-correction limit. These results demonstrate the potential of green InGaN hybrid QW-QD like VCSELs for visible-light communication (VLC) and short reach optical interconnect applications.

title: AI-Assisted Hybrid Deep Learning Model of Accurate Cardiac Health Assessment with Clinical and Demographic Markers
authors: R. Arulkumar, P.Malini, V.C. Bharathi, S.Muthulakshmi, P. Umaeswari, R.Sathya
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Electronic Devices and Intelligent Systems (ICSEDIS)
abstract: cardiovascular diseases are among the most important issues of global public health that lead to high morbidity, mortality, and healthcare spending across the globe. The accurate and timely detection of cardiac diseases is vital in not only the facilitation of early clinical management of the disease but also halting the development of the disease and alleviating the occurrence of complications that lead to mortality. The conventional diagnostic processes rely largely on the clinical knowledge of clinicians and manual assessment of diverse clinical signs, such as physiological and laboratory measurements. The current trends in the field of artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML) have brought with them the data-driven processes that can effectively analyze high-dimensional medical data and reveal complicated latent patterns that are naturally hard to detect during the standard statistical analysis. Regardless of these benefits, potentially leading to issues like overfitting, diminished generalization, and decreased model interpretability, this study suggests the implementation of an AI-assisted hybrid deep learning model that combines the deep feature representation learning algorithm with the optimized classification plans to improve the predictive robustness and stability of the model. The suggested model has a systemic method of including both the clinical parameters and demographic characteristics in order to have a health status. Through the exploitation of hybrid learning machineries, the framework will enhance diagnostic precision, reduce differences in risk evaluation, and help clinicians with sound and evidence-based decision assistance to identify early cardiac illness. The proposed hybrid deep learning framework achieved 96.4% accuracy in cardiovascular disease prediction, ensuring reliable and robust diagnosis.

title: MIND EASE+: AI based Mental Health Support System
authors: K N Sivabalan, Sajin S, Sivajith S P, Vasanth Kumar K, Vignesh S
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Electronic Devices and Intelligent Systems (ICSEDIS)
abstract: Stress, anxiety, and depression are mental health conditions that are increasingly affecting people across the world, yet many individuals still struggle to receive timely psychological help due to the stigma attached to mental health, the cost of professional care, and the limited availability of trained practitioners. This paper introduces MindEase+, an AI-powered mental health support system built to provide round-the-clock, empathetic, and personalized conversational support to users in need. The system brings together a LoRA fine-tuned LLaMA large language model for generating responses specific to the mental health domain, a multi-class emotion detection module that identifies the emotional state of the user across seven categories, and a mood tracking feature that continuously logs and examines emotional changes over time. On the architectural side, the system is supported by a web-based interface, a Spring Boot backend, a dedicated AI inference layer, and a secure relational database that stores conversation logs and mood data. Support for multiple languages and coping techniques rooted in cognitive behavioral therapy principles make the system more accessible and personalized for a wider range of users. Testing results show that the emotion detection component reached 87% accuracy, 85% precision, 84% recall, and an F1-score of 86%, performing better than standard conversational models in terms of contextual understanding and emotional appropriateness. These outcomes support the practicality of using parameter-efficient fine-tuning approaches like LoRA to build domain-focused, scalable AI systems for mental health applications.

title: Computer Vision-based Detection and Segmentation of Wind Turbine Surface Damage using YOLOv8 and SAM Models
authors: M.Balaji, P.Vijaya Lakshmi, A. Srithar, R. Manimegalai, P. Pradeep, Joshuva Arockia Dhanraj
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Electronic Devices and Intelligent Systems (ICSEDIS)
abstract: Inspection of wind turbine blades is vital for the stability and power output of clean energy technologies. The manual inspection procedures, cost a lot of money and can be dangerous in some cases. This paper presents a computer vision approach for the real-time detection and segmentation of surface defects and dirt contaminants on wind turbine blades based on high-resolution UAV images. In this work, we propose an approach based on the YOLOv8 object detection system with the Segment Anything Model (SAM) that allows for precise defect segmentation. We experimented with 4 types of YOLOv8 architectures (Nano, Small, Medium and Large) using WTSurface Damage dataset. The experimental results suggest that the YOLOv8-Small model has the best trade-off between accuracy and computational complexity with a mean Average Precision (mAP50) of 0.903 for detecting dirt and 0.755 for detecting damage, while achieving real-time performance at 148 frames per second (FPS). The SAM model performed well for damage segmentation; however, it was limited in its ability to segment dirt regions, as these were visually indistinguishable from the blade surface. Taken together these results demonstrate that UAV-based inspection integrated with state-of-the-art deep learning models provides an efficient solution for the automation of wind turbine monitoring to enable proactive maintenance approaches in mature renewable energy system.

title: Adaptive and Explainable Cybersecurity Framework for Real-Time Threat Detection
authors: Sirodhar Athyala
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Electronic Devices and Intelligent Systems (ICSEDIS)
abstract: The rate of the artificial intelligence development facilitated cyber attacks, including intelligent phishing and adaptive malware helped to lead to the reduced utility of the classical signature-based cybersecurity systems. The traditional systems have a limitation of not being able to generalize effectively to new attack patterns, which causes increased false negative and delay in threat detection particularly in the cloud setup. In a bid to curb these challenges, this paper introduces a smart and understandable cybersecurity system whose implementation can assist in real-time detection of adaptive cyber threats. The suggested system will leverage both adaptive machine learning algorithms and explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) to enhance the reliability of the detection process, and transparency of the decision-making process. The system represents complex attack behaviors and prevents false positives without signatures through behavior-driven analysis and through continuous adaptation in order to capture the complex attack behaviors. Experimental findings using publicly available cybersecurity benchmark datasets indicate that the proposed approach is accurate, lower in false negative, and explainable than the current security systems. The results establish the compliance of the principles that state that the cooperation between adaptability and explainability is essential in the evolution of credible and strong cybersecurity frameworks in the context of the cloud.

title: A Novel Soft Sensor Based on Gating Weighted Multi-Head Self-Attention LSTM Network for Dynamic Nonlinear Processes
authors: Miao Zhang, Gang Xu, Le Zhou
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Accurate measurement of quality variables is crucial for effective process monitoring and product quality control. Soft sensors are increasingly being applied to quality prediction in complex industrial processes. However, when the local dynamic nonlinearity of the process data is too strong, the existing soft sensor models still face challenges in extracting multi-scale local spatiotemporal features. Therefore, a soft sensor model based on gating weighted multi-head self-attention long short-term memory network (GWMA-LSTM) is proposed. The network utilizes multiple multi-head self-attention (MSA) mechanisms to extract the spatial features among process variables within time intervals of different scales. In order to make rational use of the temporal nature that still exists among these spatial features, they are guided to the LSTM layer for in-depth temporal feature extraction. Meanwhile, a weighted attention mechanism layer is designed to dynamically process the feature information integrated by multiple MSA layers, in order to enhance the local key feature extraction ability of the model. Furthermore, the gating mechanism is utilized to screen the information of the integrated spatiotemporal features, enhancing the model's processing ability for nonlinear processes. Finally, the experimental results on a numerical example and a sulfur recovery unit process demonstrate the effectiveness and superiority of the proposed method.

title: Beyond Data: Envisioning Semantically Aware AI-Driven Wireless Networks
authors: Mohammad Abualhayja'a, Maryam Hafeez, Syed Ali Raza Zaidi, De Mi, Mallik Tatipamula
publish: IEEE Communications Magazine
abstract: This article explores the integration of semantic AI into future wireless networks, a paradigm that enhances AI models with contextual understanding, knowledge representation, and reasoning capabilities. By leveraging ontologies, knowledge graphs (KGs), and semantic rule-based reasoning, we demonstrate how semantic AI can enable proactive network management, energy-efficient communication, and adaptive resource optimisation while addressing challenges such as data heterogeneity, interpretability, and computational overhead. We present key applications, including fault prediction, intent-based networking, and cognitive resource allocation, supported by use cases highlighting the collaboration between semantic technologies and AI-driven wireless systems. Additionally, we showcase results that highlight the validity of this approach. A KG-based system successfully applied key performance indicators (KPIs) root cause analysis by semantically linking KPIs like handover failures to network parameters (e.g., time-to-trigger adjustments).

title: WJAC-Dice: Weakly Supervised Joint Atlas Construction and Registration
authors: Wenjie Chen, Yelan Yu, Chuanyu Diao, Shunbo Hu
publish: 2026 International Conference on Generative Artificial Intelligence and Information Security (GAIIS)
abstract: To address the challenges of unsupervised registration methods, such as converging to local optima in low-contrast regions and misaligning anatomical boundaries, this chapter proposes a Joint Atlas Construction and Registration framework supervised by the Dice coefficient (WJAC-Dice). This framework incorporates segmentation labels as weak supervision signals and designs a novel registration network that integrates multi-scale spatial attention mechanisms with frequency-domain decomposition strategies. This enables the adaptive focusing on critical anatomical features, such as gray-white matter interfaces. In terms of loss function design, the Dice coefficient is innovatively integrated into the optimization objective to directly constrain the deformation field based on anatomical structure overlap, thereby overcoming the limitations of relying solely on intensity similarity. Furthermore, an indirect evaluation strategy based on atlas bridging is proposed to decouple the dependency on segmentations of blurry average atlases, achieving simultaneous improvements in both registration accuracy and atlas centrality.

title: Design of an Electronic Sub-Consumer Unit for Lights Voltage-Surge Protection
authors: Okure Tom, Rakotoarinesa Dolly Joel, Pankaj, Balaka Biswas
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Electronic Devices and Intelligent Systems (ICSEDIS)
abstract: Increasing electrical energy demand, modern lifestyles and energy usage patterns have made the world fully dependent on power systems thus, more pressure is exerted on the electrical distribution utilities to keep the power stable, reliable and ensure safety of the consumers property. In this research study, an electronic system has been designed by combining a voltage stabilizer, with voltage spike isolating unit (metering unit). This system is purposely designed for single phase alternating current (ac) protection of loads against voltage spikes by stepping down high supply voltages to the required standard range, using an auto-transformer alongside the metering unit that monitors its voltage-current output values, with an automated isolation responses to protect the loads in case of exceeded voltages and fault currents. Our design has the ability to be integrated with the circuit breakers in the single-phase ac consumer units to ensure maximum protection of voltage sensitive electronic devices such as led lights, by providing primary voltage protection and secondary fault current protection, to reduce maintenance costs. The impact of this design on protection has been demonstrated in this paper, through supplying a range of single ac voltages (400v-210v) to the system with real time visualization of voltage values and response actions to protect the loads.

title: A Two-Stage SCL–OSD Decoder for Short Polar Codes via Optimized Soft-Information Fusion
authors: Geon Choi, Namyoon Lee
publish: IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
abstract: Polar codes decoded by successive-cancellation list decoding (SCL) are a workhorse of 5G control channels, but practical hardware limits on list size leave a nontrivial gap to near–maximum-likelihood decoding (MLD) in the short blocklength regime. This paper proposes a two-stage decoder that closes this gap without paying the full cost on every frame: a moderate-list SCL decoder handles the typical case, and an ordered-statistics decoder (OSD) is invoked only when SCL fails to boost decoding performance. The central technical obstacle is the interface between the two stages—OSD needs reliable soft information to rank bit positions, while SCL is not naturally a soft-output decoder in contrast to conventional belief propagation decoding. We address this by extracting approximate soft outputs from SCL and introducing a simple but principled weighting optimization that fuses these outputs with the channel reliabilities, tuned specifically to maximize the accuracy of identifying the K most reliable positions that drive OSD performance. Simulations show that the proposed SCL–OSD cascade achieves substantial block error rate gains over conventional SCL at comparable latency, and can closely approach the random-coding union bound with practical average complexity.
