title: Learning to Debate: Optimal Stopping Strategies for Multi-Agent LLM Deliberation
authors: Xinyi Zhang, Di Hu
publish: 2026 International Conference on Generative Artificial Intelligence and Information Security (GAIIS)
abstract: Multi-agent debate has emerged as a promising paradigm for improving the reasoning capabilities of large language models (LLMs). However, the relationship between debate duration and output quality remains poorly understood. In this paper, we introduce the debate fatigue hypothesis: the notion that debate quality follows a non-monotonic trajectory, initially improving with additional rounds before eventually degrading. We propose a scaling law that models debate quality as a function of rounds: Q(T) = a • log(T + c) − b • T, where the fatigue coefficient b> 0 captures diminishing returns. Through extensive experiments with GPT-4o across 108+ debates spanning five task categories (Mathematics, Code, Logic, Science, and Finance), we validate this hypothesis with 100% confirmation rate. We further develop an adaptive stopping strategy that achieves 98.7% of oracle performance while reducing computational costs by up to 50%. Additionally, our security evaluation demonstrates that multi-agent debate reduces hallucination indicators by 61.6% and corrects 50.6% of single-agent errors, providing significant safety improvements for LLM deployment. Our findings provide both theoretical foundations and practical guidelines for deploying multi-agent debate systems efficiently in industrial applications.

title: Fire and Smoke Detection using YOLOv8 and YOLO26: A Comparative Experimental Study with Architectural Evolution Analysis
authors: Bashaer Abdullah Alsaidalani, Wael M.S. Yafooz
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Electronic Devices and Intelligent Systems (ICSEDIS)
abstract: Fire and smoke detection is a critical computer vision application for real-time safety monitoring systems. While the YOLO family of object detectors has undergone significant architectural evolution from YOLOv1 to the recent YOLO26, there remains a lack of systematic comparative evaluation between established and emerging YOLO architectures for domain-specific tasks such as fire and smoke detection. This study addresses this gap by conducting a controlled comparative experimental evaluation of YOLOv8 and YOLO26 architectures across three model scales (small, medium, and large). Additionally, it trains and evaluates under identical conditions using a custom dataset of 2,324 annotated images. The experimental results demonstrate that YOLOv8s achieved the highest overall detection accuracy with a test mAP@50 of 0.768 and mAP@50-95 of 0.547. However, YOLO26 variants exhibited competitive strengths in specific metrics: YOLO26s achieved the highest precision (0.819), YOLO26m the highest recall (0.719), and YOLO26l the highest F1-score (0.748). Additionally, this study compares performance against state-of-the-art architectures including Faster R-CNN and RT-DETR. It confirms the superiority of YOLO-based approaches for real-time fire detection. A structured analysis of the architectural evolution of YOLO detectors from 2015 to 2025 is also presented.

title: ECLO-DHSCNet for Jamming Detection and Mitigation in Cognitive Radio IoT
authors: C.C.Athavale, K.P. Patil
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Electronic Devices and Intelligent Systems (ICSEDIS)
abstract: Cognitive Radio Internet of Things (CRIoT) networks are vulnerable to jamming attacks because of their vast spectrum usage and limited capabilities of IoT devices. In such attacks, an adversary deliberately transmits interfering signals to disrupt normal communication that causes data loss and potential security risks. Detecting and preventing jamming in CRIoT environments is particularly difficult due to the constantly changing spectrum conditions. In this work, Exponential Cape Lynx Optimizer enabled Dual Horizontal Squash Capsule Network (ECLO_DHS-CapsNet) is devised to detect and mitigate jamming instance in CRIoT. Initially, CRIoT system model is simulated. Here, different types of IoT devices use wireless spectrum to communicate, while a centralized or distributed receiver continuously monitors the incoming signals for any irregularities or unusual activity. When abnormal patterns indicative of interference appears, jamming instance detection is performed using Dual Horizontal Squash Capsule Network (DHS-CapsNet), which is trained by Exponential Cape Lynx Optimizer (ECLO). The ECLO incorporates Exponentially Weighted Moving Average (EWMA) and Cape Lynx Optimizer (CLO). Once it detected the jamming instance, the system performs the mitigation process by blocklisting the malicious users. The ECLO_DHS-CapsNet is evaluated using metrics, such as accuracy, True Positive Rate (TPR), True Negative Rate (TNR), and F1-score, achieving peak values of 96.47%, 96.17%, 95.22%, and 95.21%, respectively.

title: DeepServeCB: SLO-Aware and Cost-Aware Elastic Scheduling for Serverless Multi-Tenant LLM Inference
authors: Xuexian Li, Xiayuan Liu, Zilong Wang, Zijian Xiao, Chun-Yao Hsieh, Yixue Liu
publish: 2026 International Conference on Generative Artificial Intelligence and Information Security (GAIIS)
abstract: Deploying large language model (LLM) inference services in serverless, multi-tenant environments present compounding challenges: cold start latency, GPU memory fragmentation, inter-tenant resource contention, and unpredictable tail latency. Existing systems optimize individual aspects but fail to jointly address service-level objective (SLO) compliance and cost efficiency under dynamic, heterogeneous workloads. We present DeepServeCB, an elastic scheduling framework that formulates the joint SLO--cost optimization as a contextual bandit problem. The system introduces a Request Profiler that extracts online features---prompt length, historical KV-cache hit ratio, and predicted generation length---and feeds them into a contextual bandit agent that adaptively selects batch sizes, concurrency levels, KV-cache eviction policies, and warm-standby strategies. We evaluate DeepServeCB on ShareGPT and BurstGPT traces using LLaMA-2-13B and Mixtral-8x7B models on NVIDIA A100 GPUs. Results show that DeepServeCB reduces P99 latency by 38--62% compared to state-of-the-art baselines while improving GPU utilization by 14--23% and reducing per-request cost by up to 27%, with only a modest increase in scheduling overhead.

title: BioBin360: An AI-Driven, Contactless Sanitary Waste Management System with Dual-Mode Treatment for Smart Cities
authors: Kalaivani K, Dharshini R, Harini M S, Sugapriya B
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Electronic Devices and Intelligent Systems (ICSEDIS)
abstract: Menstrual waste management in households and institutions is an urgent problem as it poses hygiene risks, manual activities, and disposal techniques that are detrimental to the environment, including open dumping or burning. In this paper, the author introduces BioBin360, a Smart City sanitary waste management system, an intelligent, non-contact, and non-contactless system. The system builds on the use of AI to classify wastes and dual-mode treatment along with IoT monitoring to make the process of disposal safe, automated, and sustainable. The users place the sanitary pads through a touch-free device where the pads are then pre-sterilized either by UV-C irradiation or disinfectant sprays to kill any pathogens and odors. A minimal convolutional neural network will identify pads as either biodegradable or non-biodegradable, and thus, it will be possible to segment pads automatically, without the human touch. Biodegradable pads are decomposed by enzymes in a controlled environment whereas synthetic pads are shredded, dried and disinfected to ensure that they are clean and small. IoT sensors are used to keep a constant check on the waste levels, temperature, humidity, smell, and health of the system, sending real-time data to a cloud-based panel where they are used to predictive maintenance and effective resources usage. BioBin360 offers the scalable, non-incineration business system enhancing the hygiene of the population, the reduction of negative environmental effects, and the achievement of sustainable Smart City sanitation systems.

title: Radar Radiation Source Sorting Based on Spatio-Temporal Graph Neural Network
authors: Fuqun Zhang, Zhengjie Fan, Mingyue Feng, Yinan Zhang, Zhihao Xu
publish: 2026 International Conference on Generative Artificial Intelligence and Information Security (GAIIS)
abstract: To address the challenge of sorting in massive, dense, and overlapping pulse signal streams, this paper proposes a radar emitter sorting method based on Spatio‑Temporal Graph Neural Network (STGNN). The method constructs a locally connected graph structure to characterize the spatial proximity between pulses, employs a Graph Attention Network (GAT) to learn local spatial correlation features, and extracts spatial feature vectors. Meanwhile, a Bidirectional Long Short‑Term Memory (Bi‑LSTM) network is utilized to process pulse descriptor word sequences for capturing temporal dependencies, combined with an attention mechanism for adaptive weighting, thereby generating temporal feature vectors. Furthermore, a linear layer is used to map the temporal features into spatial feature weights, which are multiplied element‑wise with the spatial features output by the GAT, yielding spatio‑temporally correlated features. Finally, the temporal features and the weighted fused spatio‑temporal features are concatenated and fed into a two‑layer fully connected classifier to obtain the probability distribution of emitter categories. Simulation experiments demonstrate that the proposed algorithm effectively improves the accuracy and robustness of emitter sorting in dense and complex signal environments.

title: A Rotated Object Detection Method for Campus Cracks in Complex Scenes
authors: Ruiheng Li, Wangwang Xu, Yi Di, Binghua Shi, Xiao Yang, Wanyu Qiu
publish: 2026 International Conference on Generative Artificial Intelligence and Information Security (GAIIS)
abstract: Crack detection for campus roads and walls is important for infrastructure safety, yet manual inspection is inefficient and unreliable in complex scenes. This paper proposes a campus crack detection method based on YOLOv8-OBB. A C2f-ASC fine-grained feature enhancement module is embedded into the backbone by introducing asymmetric strip convolution branches of size 1 × 3 and 3 × 1, which strengthens the representation of elongated crack edges and subtle textures. In addition, coordinate attention is introduced to improve spatial encoding and channel reweighting, thereby suppressing interference from strong light, weak light, and cluttered backgrounds. Experiments on a campus crack dataset show that the proposed method achieves a Precision of 87.2%, a Recall of 82.4%, and an mAP@0.5 of 86.8%, outperforming the baseline YOLOv8-OBB model. The method effectively improves the accuracy and robustness of crack detection in complex campus scenes.

title: Multimodal Neuroimaging-Driven Predictive Framework for Ultra-Early Detection of Parkinson’s Disease using Interpretable Gradient Boosting and Centroid-based Learning Algorithms
authors: Sai Kishore S, Tamilselvi P, Jayanhanthinhi T, Mohana Murthy P
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Electronic Devices and Intelligent Systems (ICSEDIS)
abstract: Parkinson disease (PD) is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder which impacts on both motor and cognitive functions, and quality of life in patients is greatly affected. Early diagnosis is essential to an intervention in good time, but conventional ways of diagnostics tend to detect the disease at its advanced stages. This paper introduces a new predictive analytics model of the onset of PD based on structural and functional images of the brain with clinical biomarkers. It relied on advanced machine learning algorithms, such as CatBoost, LightGBM, and k-Nearest Centroid classifiers, to detect the occurrence of subtle changes in the brain that can be indicative of the early disease progression. The MRI scans and clinical data were transformed into features and were then thoroughly preprocessed and dimensionality reduced before being trained on a model. The suggested solution showed the high levels of predictive accuracy and interpretability, displaying significant brain areas and clinical indicators that were correlated with the development of PD. The results support the capacity of AI-facilitated analytics in facilitating early detection, making care unique, and enhancing resource allocation in the field of healthcare, which provides a powerful methodology in subsequent studies of neurodegenerative disease.

title: Secure Digital Kyc Framework using Blockchain Enabled Verification Credentials and Homomorphic Encryption
authors: Dr.S.Praveena Rachel Kamala, Mahalakshmi R, Parkavi C
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Electronic Devices and Intelligent Systems (ICSEDIS)
abstract: Know Your Customer (KYC) verification is of great importance to banks, business and authorities with respect to conformity and building of trust. Traditional KYC is centralized and involves the transfer of all documents which create inefficiencies, privacy invasion, and lag time particularly in corporate and cross-border verification. To address these barriers, we suggest PRISM-KYC, a privacy-aware and security-conscious digital KYC. Our approach to selective extraction (just relevant fields) of document(s), e.g., turnover or tax IDs of documents of arbitrary length, uses Transformer-based document intelligence and Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG). Sensitive data undergo encryption using Homomorphic Encryption (HE), whereby data verification/checking is done without direct data decryption. Output of verification is kept on the blockchain in the form of Verifiable Credentials (VC) and has tamper-proof and audit records. The new component of PRISM-KYC is the collaboration between AI-prompted selective extraction, blockchain-verified computation, and encrypted credentials, which provides the opportunity to have decentralized, efficient, and privacy-preserving KYC. Experimental findings indicate a significant increase in efficiency without any data leakage, which is applicable to corporate onboarding, mergers, and compliance in cross-border.

title: Machine Learning-Enhanced Surrogate Modeling for Marine Propeller Performance Prediction using CFD
authors: Md. Mashud Karim, Mahin Hasan Moon
publish: 2025 IEEE Ocean Engineering Technology and Innovation Conference: Management and Conservation for Sustainable and Resilient Marine and Coastal Resources (OETIC)
abstract: We present a reproducible, physics-aware surrogate modeling workflow that fuses low-cost virtual-disk computational fluid dynamics (CFD) with model-scale open-water measurements to rapidly predict marine propeller open-water performance. Selfpropulsion virtual-disk Reynolds-averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS) simulations were performed at three rotational speeds (7.5, 10.0, $\mathbf{1 2. 0} \mathbf{~ r p s}$) and augmented with experimental data from the National Maritime Research Institute (NMRI) JBC-687 covering an advance-ratio sweep $\mathrm{J}=0.10-0.80$. The merged dataset was used to train and compare four regression surrogates: Random Forest (RF), XGBoost, Support Vector Regression (RBF-SVR), and a feedforward neural network (multilayer perceptron, MLP). The RF baseline achieved strong test-set accuracy (e.g., $\mathrm{R}^{2} \approx 0.96,0.94$, 0.91 for $\mathrm{KT}, \mathrm{KQ}$, and $\eta 0$, respectively) with maximum absolute coefficient errors below 3.5%; XGBoost andMLP show comparable performance. Inference is efficient (on theorder of $10^{-2}-10^{-1} \mathrm{~s}$ per sample), yielding a runtime speedup> $1 \times 10^{5} \times$ versus full 3D RANS. We quantify predictive uncertainty using RF ensemble variance and assess feature relevance via permutation importance, which confirms advance ratio J as the dominant predictor. The framework enables engineering-level accuracy for rapid design-space exploration and is documented with solver settings, mesh-convergence guidance, and ML hyperparameters to support reproducibility.

title: A Hybrid CNN–SVR based Decision Support System for Road Edge and Shoulder Repair
authors: Milcah Blessy I, G. Jaspher W Kathrine, S. Salaja
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Electronic Devices and Intelligent Systems (ICSEDIS)
abstract: Erosion of the road edges poses significant safety and financial challenges to the transportation agencies, and the process of human inspection remains tedious, inconsistent and difficult to reach. This paper proposes a hybrid decision support system (DSS) based on a convolutional neural network–support vector regression (CNN–SVR) for automated assessment of road edge conditions and repair planning in order to overcome this constraint. In order to estimate a continuous road edge severity index that ranges from 0 to 1, the suggested framework combines support vector regression with MobileNetV2-based deep feature extraction. This index is then further divided into actionable maintenance levels. Model robustness was assessed using 5-fold stratified cross-validation on a curated dataset of 605 real-world road edge photos classified as Clear, Moderate, and Severe. With a standard deviation of 1.70%, the suggested model’s average accuracy was 98.68%. Additionally, it achieved an F1-score, accuracy, and recall score of 0.987, with mean absolute error (MAE) and root mean square error (RMSE) of 0.093 and 0.099, respectively. The suggested CNN–SVR model performs better than a number of basic machine learning and deep learning techniques, according to comparative data and allows determining the conditions of road edges accurately and in a scalable and real-time manner, which is why it is appropriate to be deployed in intelligent transportation and smart infrastructure management systems

title: Optimized Route-based Energy Consumption Prediction for Electric City Buses using a CNN2D Framework
authors: K.Praveen Kumar, B. Anurag, R. Manas Kumar, CH. Mythri Reddy, Suma S, G.Swarnalatha
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Electronic Devices and Intelligent Systems (ICSEDIS)
abstract: The rapid introduction of electric buses to the urban transport networks has presented the need of extremely precise forecasting of energy use that is conducive to the route planning and provision of charging sites in a more efficient manner. A significant obstacle to the electric buses is that it can take a long time to charge these buses, which is primarily an operational factor; hence, proper estimation of the energy requirement becomes extremely important in doing less downtime provision and proper management of the fleets. The paper presents a framework that relies on data to forecast the route-wise energy used by the city buses by using artificial intelligence techniques of machine learning and deep learning. It is argued that the suggested method relies on the parameters that will be operational and also associated with the route that have been navigated through the real-world datasets of buses, followed by the feature selecting that is based on the neighborhood and finding out the attributes that will be most relevant to the energy usage. They implement different machine learning models that rely on regression like Linear Regression, Random Forest, Support vector machine, Artificial Neural Networks, and Gaussian Process Regression and their performance is evaluated using such widespread measures as the R 2 score, Root Mean Square Error (RMSE), and Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE). Furthermore, a two-dimensional convolutional neural network (CNN2D) model is proposed as an additional one that learns feature representations automatically and finds complex features representations. The findings of the experiments indicate that CNN2D model is far superior to the conventional machine learning models as it has a maximum R 2 score of 0.98 and a smaller error in predictions. It is possible to not only estimate the energy usage of the unidentified routes through the proposed framework but also to create intelligent strategies of managing the electric bus fleet in the smart cities.

title: Long-tail multi-class counterfactual explanation
authors: Shiji Chen
publish: 2026 International Conference on Generative Artificial Intelligence and Information Security (GAIIS)
abstract: With the rapid development of interpretable machine learning, counterfactual explanation has become an effective method to reveal the decision-making mechanism of classification models. Aiming at the problems of imbalanced distribution in long-tailed multi-class classification tasks, such as scarce tail-class samples, ambiguous feature distribution, and significant head-class bias, as well as the defects of existing counterfactual explanation methods including blind target class selection, insufficient authenticity of generated samples, and weak causal discriminability, this paper proposes an enhanced counterfactual explanation method for long-tailed multi-class classification models. Firstly, a long-tailed-aware intelligent target class routing mechanism is designed, which integrates four key indicators: class frequency weight, inter-class semantic similarity, model confusion relationship, and prediction uncertainty, to adaptively select the target class with high explanation value and rationality for the instance to be explained, so as effectively to solve the difficulty of determining counterfactual targets in long-tailed scenarios. Secondly, a consistent constraint for head-class bias suppression is constructed to forcibly weaken the interference and suppression of dominant features of head classes on tail-class recognition, enabling counterfactual explanations to reveal the real decision-making causality and improve the discriminability and reliability of explanations. Finally, comparative experiments are conducted on multiple classic public long-tailed tabular datasets, and a variety of metrics are introduced for comprehensive evaluation, including effectiveness, L1 distance, generation time, and tail-class success rate. Experimental results show that the proposed method outperforms existing representative methods in counterfactual generation quality, explanation effectiveness, feature modification extent, and running efficiency, and can provide more accurate, credible, and efficient interpretability support for long-tailed multi-class classification models in practical applications.

title: 2026 Fourth International Conference on Distributed Computing and High Performance Computing (DCHPC)
authors: N/A
publish: N/A

title: Code-Weight Sphere Decoding
authors: Yubeen Jo, Geon Choi, Yongjune Kim, Namyoon Lee
publish: IEEE Wireless Communications Letters
abstract: Ultra-reliable low-latency communications (URLLC) demand high-performance error-correcting codes and decoders in the finite blocklength regime. This letter introduces a novel two-stage near-maximum likelihood (near-ML) decoding framework applicable to any linear block code. Our approach first employs a low-complexity initial decoder. If this initial stage fails a cyclic redundancy check, it triggers a second stage: the proposed code-weight sphere decoding (WSD). WSD iteratively refines the codeword estimate by exploring a localized sphere of candidates constructed from pre-computed low-weight codewords. This strategy adaptively minimizes computational overhead at high signal-to-noise ratios while achieving near-ML performance, especially for low-rate codes. Extensive simulations demonstrate that our two-stage decoder provides an excellent trade-off between decoding reliability and complexity, establishing it as a promising solution for next-generation URLLC systems.

title: Edge Computing Framework for Healthcare Applications
authors: Prabh Deep Singh, Jatinderpal Singh, Sonia F Panesar, Narinder Kaur, Nidhi, Kiran Deep Singh
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Electronic Devices and Intelligent Systems (ICSEDIS)
abstract: The emergence of smart health care networks and the Internet of Medical Things (IoMT) has offered the opportunity to observe patients and provide them with individual care in a way that has never existed before. However, the medical applications’ latency sensitivity is a significant challenge to the traditional cloud-centric systems in that such delays in the processing of vital health information can have the consequence of compromising patient safety and clinical decision-making. Real-time medical scenarios such as cardiac arrest detection, fall alarms, and vital sign anomalies cannot be handled through centralized cloud architecture in the near future due to the need to respond within seconds. This paper discusses edge computing as one way of minimizing the latency of a healthcare application by doing distributed computing at the edges of the network. We propose the Healthcare Edge Priority Scheduling Algorithm (HEPSA) which plans activities related to the medical field based on the clinical urgency and intelligently broadcasts the information to various computational layers. We simulate four system configurations under a number of patient scenarios and compare end-to-end latency, response time, energy consumption, and network consumption with the iFogSim simulator. Experimentally, HEPSA reduces average response time by 73 percent of cloud-only architecture and latency by 45 percent of straightforward edge implementations. The overall effects are positive patient outcome because of the reduced emergency detection time and reduced clinical response time.

title: U-Net Refined Iterative Image reconstruction Algorithm for Compressed Sensing via Adaptive Gradient optimization
authors: Ranji Sankar S, Nakkeeran R
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Electronic Devices and Intelligent Systems (ICSEDIS)
abstract: This study introduces a novel adaptive gradient optimization based compressed sensing framework for efficient image reconstruction. The existing optimization-based image reconstruction algorithms generally have a slow convergence speed and high computational complexity, while the network-based methods may have limited adaptability to different signal distributions and heavily under-sampled data. To address these limitations, we integrate model-based optimization with data-driven deep learning in a staged reconstruction framework to leverage the complementary strengths of both approaches. The image recovery problem is formulated as an optimization task to recover the original image from the compressed measurements. The proposed framework includes four phases: compression, initial reconstruction, adaptive gradient-based optimization, and final refinement with a U-Net, designed to ensure stable convergence and robust performance across sampling conditions. The adaptive optimization process can effectively promote the convergence stability, while the refinement network can further improve the structural consistency and remove the reconstruction artifacts. A series of experiments on various benchmark datasets, including OST300, CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and SET14,with different sampling ratios have been conducted to verify the effectiveness of the proposed approach. For example, the proposed method can achieve a PSNR of 31.21 dB and an SSIM of 0.923 on the OST300 dataset with a sampling ratio of 0.25, which clearly validates the effective use of image sparsity for robust image reconstruction.

title: Substrate-Engineered Low-SAR Z-Shaped Patch Antenna for Human-Safe Wireless Systems
authors: Santini Swetha A, Vishali M, Avudaiammal R
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Electronic Devices and Intelligent Systems (ICSEDIS)
abstract: The high rate of mobile phone spread has made the populace greatly exposed to electromagnetic radiation thereby raising the issue of the possible health hazards posed by the presence of the high Specific Absorption Rate (SAR) levels. Specifically, cheap unbranded equipment does not have clear radiation requirements, putting the users at greater risk. This research seeks to solve this problem by designing and analyzing a patch antenna of Z-shaped (mid-GHz band) electromagnetic radiation to limit the exposure of humans to the radiation. Fr4 and silicone rubber in the form of two substrate materials are studied to determine their impact on the aspects of radiation properties and absorption. It is characterized by a dimensionally optimized antenna which obtains a stable impedance which matches and controlled radiation patterns and minimizes SAR. With simulation-based evaluation, active resonance in the target frequency range and tolerable absorption at regulatory boundaries have proved to be effective. The results will lead to safer antenna design focus and will help in creating knowledge regarding the choice of substrate in decreasing the radiation exposure in wireless communication systems.

title: Real-time Stochastic Predictive Control for Liquid Cooling Battery Thermal Management of EVs
authors: Baolin Ma, Lulu Guo, Hong Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Transportation Electrification
abstract: Battery thermal management (BTM) system is essential for battery power supply and vehicle driving safety, especially in the extreme weather. In light of the substantial computational load imposed by long time prediction optimization of high-order nonlinear BTM dynamic system and conflict between BTM actuator quick response and battery temperature slow changing characters, a multi-time scale hierarchical structure based on the intermediate variable method is introduced for a secondary liquid cooling BTM system to accelerate computational efficiency and improve system energy-saving in this paper. Considering the challenges of accessing precise future predictive vehicle information directly, a stochastic speed prediction based on the Markov process for daily route scenarios is embedded in a hierarchical control structure. Simulation results show that it can achieve up to 12.6% energy-saving improvement and 82.6% computation time reduction, compared to centralized structure. Especially, the performance hardly deteriorates under the introduction of stochastic speed prediction. Additionally, the real-time character of the proposed control strategy is validated on the high-performance target machine, and embedded onboard system. Furthermore, the proposed control strategy demonstrates consistent performance across diverse conditions, highlighting its inherent robustness.

title: FedAdaptQ: Adaptive Communication-Efficient Federated Learning for IoT and Edge Devices
authors: Atefeh Hemmati, Shirin Abbasi, Amir Masoud Rahmani
publish: 2026 Fourth International Conference on Distributed Computing and High Performance Computing (DCHPC)
abstract: Federated Learning (FL) is a relatively new concept, one that allows distributed training of models without having to rely on raw data being centralized. FL preserves privacy and fosters cooperation in learning. However, across the Internet of Things (IoT) and edge environments, a significant challenge remains: the high communication overhead that results from frequent model updates, especially under heterogeneous and non-IID data. To solve this, we propose an adaptive, communication-efficient framework that implements dynamic sparsification together with fine-grained layer-wise quantization-and we call it FedAdaptQ. The sparsification rate is adjusted as a function of training loss and gradient variance-sending more information in the earlier, less stable stages, and less in the later stable stages. At the same time, large gradients are selected by local metrics and quantized to conserve communication cost while maintaining accuracy. Experiments under non-IID frameworks manifest that the proposed method achieved competitive accuracy against FedAvg. These results raise the promise of FedAdaptQ for resource-constrained environments such as IoT and mobile devices.

title: Towards CSI Localization at Scale: Unsupervised Representation Learning for Fingerprint Retrieval
authors: Tianyu Zhang, Dongheng Zhang, Yan Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Mobile Computing
abstract: As learning-based WiFi localization scales to large deployments, obtaining more labeled data becomes increasingly challenging due to prohibitive annotation costs. Traditional methods that drive model training with labeled datasets face a fundamental limitation: their performance is closely coupled with the labeled data and degrades under label scarcity. To address this challenge, we propose leveraging naturally collected unlabeled data to decouple model training from costly annotations. Our approach leverages unsupervised representation learning for model training; the learned representations preserve spatial proximity, which in turn allows non-parametric retrieval to achieve accurate localization. Specifically, we advance both the training strategy and the model design to address the challenges of large-scale localization, making two contributions:(i) an unsupervised training strategy that exploits timestamps as natural supervision signals, combined with mutually exclusive and random AP masking to mitigate shortcut learning; and (ii) an attention-based architecture for large-scale snapshot localization, which models visible channel observations from multiple APs as a set and encodes them with learnable AP embeddings, thereby accommodating dynamic visibility without explicit coordinate encoding. We evaluate on three datasets of increasing difficulty, spanning robot to handheld collection, customized to commercial devices, and region- to building-scale environments. Experimental results across seven scenarios show that our tailored architecture reduces mean localization error by 9.8% compared with existing designs, and our unsupervised method achieves a further 10.4% improvement over prior unsupervised approaches. Notably, when combined with non-parametric fingerprint retrieval, unsupervised learning even surpasses supervised baselines under label-scarce regimes, with gains of up to 10.5%.

title: An Embedded IoT Node with Vision–Audio Sensor Fusion for Distributed UAV Detection
authors: Huang-Chen Lee, Li-Lun Wu, Jui-Tung Hsu
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: Unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) intrusions near airports, military bases, and critical infrastructures have raised growing safety concerns. Conventional radar-based solutions provide accurate detection but remain costly, power-intensive, and impractical for wide-area distributed deployment. Vision- and audio-based approaches offer lower-cost alternatives, yet most existing studies rely on centralized processing or high-performance hardware, which limits scalability in real-world Internet of Things (IoT) applications. This paper proposes the IoT drone intrusion detector (DID), a low-cost, microcontroller unit (MCU)-level embedded device designed as a distributed sensing node. The DID integrates vision and audio modules and applies a sensor fusion strategy to improve robustness against environmental noise and extend the effective detection range to 30 m. To further enhance adaptability, a system-level dynamic learning mechanism is introduced that updates the detection model when persistent false alarms occur in deployment. Experimental results from a two-station field deployment demonstrate that the proposed sensor fusion approach improved UAV detection accuracy from 72.5% (vision only) and 87.5% (audio only) to 95%. In addition, the dynamic learning mechanism effectively mitigated false alarms, raising drone-class precision from 76% to 96% and enhancing robustness under long-term environmental variations. With its compact design, low power consumption, and low production cost, the DID is suitable for rooftop or elevated installation and can function as a scalable node in IoT-based distributed UAV detection networks.

title: A micro-motion feature extraction method for rotor UAVs based on sinusoidal frequency modulation basis under strong clutter background
authors: Hang Yuan, Ying Luo, Hai-lin Li, Jia Liang, Si-yuan Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: The micro-motion characteristics of rotor UAVs reflect the small movements and attitude changes during flight, which can provide important information for identification and distinction. Existing methods mainly utilize the echo frequency-domain representation to extract the micro-motion features of rotor UAVs. However, the performance of such methods deteriorates sharply under strong clutter background, because echo frequency domain is submerged by noise. In this article, a micro motion feature extraction method for rotor UAVs based on sinusoidal frequency modulation basis under strong clutter background is proposed. Firstly, the projection coefficient properties of rotor UAVs echoes on sinusoidal frequency modulated basis are studied. The expression and statistical properties of the coefficients are derived. The maximum variance of the projection coefficients on the sinusoidal frequency modulation basis is used to extract the target rotation frequency and the number of blades. Then, the blade length is extracted according to the coefficient properties. Finally, the effectiveness of the proposed algorithm is verified by the simulation and measured data.

title: Evaluation of Ice Crystal Scattering Models Using Polarimetric Observations
authors: Yizhen Meng, Lei Bi
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Ice clouds are an essential component of the Earth’s atmosphere, and their radiative effects strongly depend on the morphology of ice crystals. To identify ice crystal scattering models that are representative and robust for remote sensing and atmospheric modeling applications, we systematically evaluate the performance of nine established ice habit models (i.e., plate, 5-plate aggregate, 10-plate aggregate, droxtal, 8-column aggregate, hollow bullet rosette, solid bullet rosette, hollow column and solid column) along with latitude-dependent super-ellipsoidal schemes. Model performance is assessed by comparing simulated polarized radiance against measurements from the third-generation POLarization and Directionality of the Earth’s Reflectances instrument (POLDER-3). Results show that traditional ice habit models exhibit pronounced latitude dependence. Notably, the models with higher structural complexity (e.g., hollow bullet rosettes and roughened plates) show reduced latitude sensitivity and improved agreement with observations. Increasing surface roughness also improves model performance, particularly in low-latitude zones. In contrast, the mixed super-ellipsoidal scheme consistently outperforms all habit-based models, showing lower RMSE values, weaker dependence on latitude and season, and more uniform agreement across scattering angles. This likely stems from the fact that a limited set of idealized ice habits cannot fully capture the natural variability and complexity of ice crystals. These results highlight the applicability of the super-ellipsoid representation as a robust surrogate scheme for ice cloud remote sensing and its potential value for improving climate-related applications.

title: Application of Synchronized Swept-Sine Technique in CMUT Characterization Using Laser-Doppler Vibrometry
authors: Tönnis Trittler, Sören Köble, Julian Kober, Marco Kircher, Johannes Fankhänel, Cornelius Kühnöl, Paul-Henry Franz Koop, Richard Nauber, Henning Heuer, Moritz Herzog
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Ultrasonics, Ferroelectrics, and Frequency Control
abstract: Capacitive Micromachined Ultrasonic Transducers (CMUTs) provide a path to mass-produced cost-efficient transducers for clinical imaging and wearables. However, their inherent nonlinearities, especially at high excitation voltages, can degrade performance and image quality. This requires fast non-contact characterization of individual CMUT transducers directly on a wafer. This work demonstrates the first application of the Synchronized Swept-Sine Technique (SST) in Laser-Doppler Vibrometry (LDV) for comprehensive characterization of CMUTs on individual cell level. In the SST, the extraction of both linear and nonlinear harmonic transfer functions from a single measurement is possible by excitation with an exponential chirp and application of an inverse filter. Using a scanning LDV system, we characterized individual cells in a 64-cell CMUT array (50 μm diameter) across a 1...16 MHz frequency range in both air and oil. The method reveals quantitative spectral data, including resonance at 6.4 MHz and second-harmonic distortion at –11.8 dB relative to the fundamental for the investigated CMUT. Results demonstrate significant cell-to-cell variations in harmonic transfer functions and reveal nonlinear behavior even in non-excited cells due to inter-cell acoustic coupling. These findings establish SST-LDV as a powerful complementary technique to electrical impedance measurements. The method provides spatially resolved mechanical insights that are crucial for the development of CMUTs, model validation, and potentially, quality control in high-throughput MEMS manufacturing.

title: Cross Hyper-beamforming
authors: Chun-Hsien Chiang, Meng-Lin Li
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Ultrasonics, Ferroelectrics, and Frequency Control
abstract: Delay-and-sum beamformation (DAS) is the most common image reconstruction algorithm for ultrasound imaging. Its predictability, robustness, and low computational complexity make it a compromising algorithm even with lateral resolution being diffraction-limited and high sidelobe levels. Hyper-beamformation (HBF) has recently been proposed to improve lateral resolution with computational complexity similar to DAS. Besides, an integration of HBF and plane-wave compounding (HBF-PWC) has been proposed for ultrafast imaging. However, HBF-PWC might create a false-peak artifact when two-point targets were closely spaced, hindering its lateral-resolution improvement and making it only suitable for application with sparse data, i.e., ultrasound localization microscopy. In this study, we propose a novel beamforming algorithm –cross hyper-beamforming (XHBF), cross-compounding plane wave channel data from two opposite transmitting angles, and thereby creating a narrower hyper-beam, which removes the false-peak artifact in HBF-PWC and dramatically improves the resolvability when separating closely spaced point targets while retaining low computational complexity comparable to DAS. We also explore the fact that XHBF can significantly suppress grating-lobe artifacts.

title: Electro-Thermal Integrated CC-CV Charging Control for EV Batteries
authors: Shriraam R R, Cephas I
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Electronic Devices and Intelligent Systems (ICSEDIS)
abstract: Constant Current Constant Voltage (CC-CV) charging method is a common method of charging a lithium-ion battery, as it is a simple and reliable technique. This process is done in two stages, in the first stage, a constant current is applied to the cell, and this is continued until terminal voltage is equal to its maximum value, usually approximately 4.1 V in the case of most lithium-ion chemistries. At this point when the battery reaches that voltage, the second step starts. In this case, the voltage remains constant and the current gradually decreases with a decrease in the distance of the battery approaching full charge. This process that consists of two steps is effective in charging the battery more effectively and prolongs the battery life. It also helps in keeping the battery secure by preventing it to be overcharged or become too hot. The main contribution of our work is that a single electro-thermal CC-CV simulation framework with control and thermal feedback is developed instead of the introduction of a new charging control algorithm. The framework allows thorough consideration of voltage, current, temperature, and state-of-charge coupling of electric-vehicle battery management systems through the use of electrical dynamics, thermal dynamics, and CC-CV control in a single MATLAB/Simulink. Simulation results demonstrate approximately 10% reduction in charging time and improved thermal stability with temperature rise limited to less than 3 K compared with conventional CC-CV charging.

title: Research on Corona Loss Characteristics of DC Positive Polarity Conductors
authors: Shilong Huang, Yudong Zhao, Yunpeng Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industry Applications
abstract: Due to the fixed polarity of DC transmission line, the charge is distributed throughout the electrode space, and the line configuration has a significant impact on the corona loss. Therefore, based on the high-altitude corona cage in Ping'an, Xining, Qinghai, this study carried out the corona loss measurement of different types of positive four bundle conductors. The finite element simulation software was used to build the model of the conductor in corona cage, and the influencing factors of the corona loss of the four bundle conductor in corona cage were analyzed. The comparison between calculated values and experimental values verified the accuracy of the calculation model. At the same time, the actual conductor-to-ground structure model was built, corona loss and ionic flow field related parameters of the positive single conductor with different overhead ground conductor configurations, the homopolar line, the two bundle conductor with different spacing between sub-conductors were obtained, which can provide data reference and technical support for the construction and development of HVDC transmission line in high-altitude area.

title: Land Surface Temperature and Emissivity Retrieval from Multiple-Channel Thermal Infrared Images with High Spatial Resolution and Wide Swath
authors: Fengguang Li, Huazhong Ren, Songyi Lin, Ruoxin Zhu, Yanhua Zhao, Yunzhu Tao, Ruixue Ma, Zixiao Hu, Jianbin Lin, Rongyuan Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Land surface temperature (LST) serves as a key parameter for analyzing surface energy balance and hydrological cycles, driving the need for high-accuracy, high-spatial-resolution LST products in Earth system science. The Wide Thermal Infrared Imager (WTI) onboard China’s GF-5 01A satellite provides four-channel thermal infrared data with a wide swath (over 1500 km), high spatial resolution (better than 100 m at nadir), and a two-day revisit cycle, integrating the extensive coverage (like MODIS and VIIRS) with the high spatial resolution characteristics (like Landsat-8/9). This study developed multiple LST retrieval approaches for WTI data, including Temperature–Emissivity Separation (TES) algorithm, Split-Window (SW) algorithm, and the hybrid algorithm. Three kinds of algorithms demonstrated promising accuracy in simulation experiments, and were applied to real WTI images collected throughout the year of 2024 in different seasons over China. Validation against ground measurements from the HiWATER network showed that a 3-channel hybrid model algorithm TSW3T achieved optimal performance, with an overall RMSE of 2.46 K and Bias of −0.15 K. Four specific sites (DSL, HHZ, SDZ, and YKZ) maintained RMSE values below 2.0 K, demonstrating satisfactory accuracy. Specifically, at high-altitude sites, the 3-channel algorithms consistently outperformed their 4-channel counterparts. The TSW3T algorithm delivered the best validation results, achieving an RMSE of 2.22 K. Of the selected 93 matching points, the cross-comparison between TSW3T-derived LST and MODIS LST products showed an RMSE of 1.59 K and Bias of −0.17 K under an average overpass time difference of 22 minutes between WTI and MODIS. These findings confirm the robustness of the TSW3T for generating high-spatial-resolution LST and emissivity products from GF-5 01A WTI thermal infrared data.

title: Edge-Optimized Pothole Detection using Lightweight YOLO and LiDAR-Vision Fusion
authors: Pramod Mehra, Geetanjali Bisht, Deepak Kumar Chohan, Sumit Saklani, Rohan Verma
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Electronic Devices and Intelligent Systems (ICSEDIS)
abstract: Widespread potholes, a leading cause of road accidents, vehicle damage and infrastructure degradation are factors complicating urban transportation and smart city management. Conventional vision-based pothole detection approaches are susceptible to variations in illumination, shadows and low-contrast road textures whereas purely 3D sensing methods are computationally expensive to deploy on an edge for real-time detection. Proposed Framework for Potholes Detection and Its Severity Using Lightweight YOLOv8-Nano in Edge Implementation Based on a refined LiDAR-vision hybrid framework, this paper describes an optimized method with respect to edge application for detection of potholes and estimating their severity using the lightweight version of the YOLO satellite model: YOLOv8-Nano. The system realizes potholes detection and measurement of geometric characteristics such as depth and spatial extent by integrating RGB images with LiDAR depth data. For the training and evaluation of the model, we utilize Pothole-600, KITTI and synchronized RGB-LiDAR datasets. We compute and evaluate on the data of our reconstruction dataset, experimentally demonstrating that our network can reach 93.8% detection accuracy, 1.5 cm depth estimation error along with 32 ms per frame inference speed timely as each image must be processed real times. It can also flag potholes as low, medium or high severity and use the data to inform predictive road maintenance within smart city and intelligent transportation systems.

title: Multi-Beam Conformal Leaky-Wave Antenna Fed by an Integrated Quasi-Optical System
authors: Michael Dittman, Ethan Neitzke, Brian Wright, John Papapolymerou, Mauro Ettorre
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Antennas and Propagation
abstract: This paper presents a fully conformal antenna system composed of an integrated multi-layer quasi-optical system and a conformal leaky-wave antenna (CLWA). The conformal system enables simultaneous beam steering in the E- and H-principal planes, multi-beam capability, and mitigation of geometric defocusing. The work represents a novel integration of quasi-optical structures with CLWAs. The CLWA is constructed from an array of slots on a double-grounded substrate illuminated by the conformal quasi-optical system. The antenna system operates in the band of 22 GHz to 26 GHz with a design frequency of 24.15 GHz. The radiation patterns in the E- and H-planes of the CLWA are controlled by the leaky-wave mode and the quasi-optical system, respectively. The quasi-optical system is illuminated by seven input ports enabling azimuthal beam switching. The spacing between consecutive slot elements of the CLWA has been engineered to compensate for the defocusing induced by the conformal shape, such that the central feed horn in the focus of the quasi-optical system radiates broadside at 24.15 GHz. Numerical results are validated by a prototype in printed circuit board (PCB) technology. The input ports feature a reflection coefficient ≤ −10 dB in the band of 19.75 GHz to 25.50 GHz, corresponding to a fractional bandwidth of 25.41%. The measured gain for the broadside beam is 23.35 dBi at 24.15 GHz with a radiation efficiency of 65.36%. The antenna can cover an angular range of approximately 33° in elevation via frequency steering and a maximum of ±36.5° in azimuth via beam switching.

title: ROSE: Extended Evaluation of RObust and SEcure Black-Box DNN Watermarking
authors: Kassem Kallas, Teddy Furon
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: Deep neural networks (DNNs) are valuable industrial assets requiring reliable intellectual-property protection. This paper extends ROSE [1], a black-box watermarking protocol for MLaaS settings that quantifies ownership through statistical rarity (expressed as R = −log<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>(p), in bits) and adversarial computational work. ROSE binds trigger-label pairs to a secret key through a hash-based mechanism, limiting a posteriori forgery and supporting formal reasoning about verification strength. We present an expanded theoretical analysis together with an extensive empirical evaluation across multiple image-classification datasets and architectures, including CNNs, ResNets, and Vision Transformers. Experiments show that ROSE preserves primary task accuracy at low trigger fractions, achieves high watermark recovery, and yields rarity values reaching several hundred bits. The method is evaluated under a broad range of model and input-level perturbations, including fine-tuning, pruning, quantization, image transformations, and sanitizers such as Neural Cleanse and Neural Laundering. In double-watermarking settings, overwriting attempts introduce ambiguity only at the cost of substantial accuracy loss, thereby preserving the practical verifiability of the original claim. Efficiency measurements indicate low overhead: embedding remains comparable to standard training and verification incurs minimal latency. More precisely, the verification cost scales primarily with the number of trigger evaluations rather than directly with model size. While this suggests favorable protocol-level scalability, the present empirical study is restricted to the image-classification architectures evaluated in this paper. Overall, ROSE provides a practical black box watermarking framework that combines strong ownership verifiability, empirical robustness, and low verification overhead in the studied setting.
